• Pyroptosis is a type of programmed cell death that is dependent on caspase-1 activation and results in the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β and IL-18. It is initiated in response to infection by intracellular pathogens and can be triggered by the activation of pattern recognition receptors such as NOD-like receptors.

subject: Pathology
question: Which caspase is not typically involved in the process of pyroptosis, a form of cell death associated with microbial infection and inflammatory response?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Caspase 1 is a central enzyme in the pyroptotic pathway , responsible for the cleavage of pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-1β and initiating the pyroptotic cell death .
• Option C. Caspase 4 is implicated in the detection of bacterial endotoxins and can initiate noncanonical pyroptosis upon sensing these intracellular signals.
• Option D. Similar to caspase 4, caspase 5 is involved in the noncanonical pyroptosis pathway and participates in immune defense against intracellular pathogens .
• Educational Objective:
• Caspase 3 , does not play a role in pyroptosis but is a crucial mediator of apoptosis .

subject: Pathology
question:
A middle aged man 45-year-old man presents with increasing abdominal girth and yellow discoloration of his skin and sclera. He had hepatomegaly and jaundice on physical examination. A Prussian blue stain of a liver biopsy is shown in the image. What is the major intracellular iron storage protein in this patient’s hepatocytes?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/10/picture16.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bilirubin is a breakdown product of hemoglobin and is not a storage form of iron.
• Option B. Haptoglobin binds free hemoglobin released from erythrocytes , not iron directly, and is not a storage protein for iron within cells.
• Option C. Hemoglobin is the oxygen-carrying protein found in red blood cells , not an iron storage protein in hepatocytes.
• Educational Objective:
• Hemosiderin, is the major iron storage complex within cells . Its accumulation, indicated by Prussian blue staining in a liver biopsy , is indicative of iron overload and is commonly seen in conditions like hemochromatosis.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is NOT true about free radical injury?
options: It is an auto catalytic reaction
Hydroxyl radical is the most powerful free radical
Vitamins A, B and C are powerful antioxidants
Intracellular ratio of oxidized glutathione (GSSG) to reduced glutathione (GSH) reflects the oxidative state of the cell.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Vitamins A, B and C are powerful antioxidants
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Free radical reactions can propagate themselves in an autocatalytic manner.
• Option B. The hydroxyl radical is indeed one of the most reactive free radicals.
• Option D. The intracellular ratio of oxidized glutathione (GSSG) to reduced glutathione (GSH) reflects the oxidative state of the cell.This ratio is a key indicator of cellular oxidative stress.
• Educational Objective:
• While Vitamins A and C are antioxidants,  Vitamin B does not serve as an antioxidant.

subject: Pathology
question: A 60-year-old man presents with Hodgkin lymphoma undergoes 3 sessions of radiotherapy. Microscopic examination in the field of radiation reveals numerous acidophilic bodies and small cells with pyknotic nuclei. Which of the following proteins most likely played a key role in triggering radiation-induced cell death in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cytochrome P450 enzymes are involved in drug metabolism and are not directly associated with the cellular response to DNA damage .
• Option C. β-Catenin is involved in cell signaling and adhesion, with roles in the Wnt signaling pathway and not directly in DNA damage response.
• Option D. E-Cadherin is a cell adhesion molecule important for maintaining cell-to-cell junctions and is not involved in the DNA damage response.
• Educational Objective:
• Following DNA damage from radiotherapy , p53 assesses the severity of genomic abnormalities and determines whether to activate repair mechanisms or proceed to apoptosis if the damage is beyond repair .

subject: Pathology
question: A 20-year-old man goes on a fun trip to Goa and spends 6 hours on the beach in day time. The next day he has a darker complexion. His skin does not show warmth, erythema, or tenderness. His skin tone fades to its original appearance within a month. Which of the following substances contributes the most to the biochemical process leading to these skin changes?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Heme containing iron as part of hemosiderin from breakdown of red blood cells can impart a brownish colour, but this is typically local from trauma or more global as part of an iron storage disease such as hemochromatosis . Iron oxide is also used in tattoo pigments .
• Option C. Homogentisic acid can be part of the process of the rare disease alkaptonuria, in which a black pigment is deposited in connective tissues
• Option D. Glycogen in quantity is starch-like and imparts a paler colour to organs in which it is stored in excess . It does not involve the skin.
• Educational objective:
• Tyrosine is the key substrate in melanin synthesis , which is enhanced by UV exposure , leading to tanning of the skin . Tyrosinase is the enzyme responsible for the conversion of tyrosine to dihydroxyphenylalanine (DOPA) in the melanin synthesis pathway.

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old ingests a large amount of paracetamol and 12 hours later develops malaise and abdominal pain. Laboratory studies show an elevated ALT. N-acetylcysteine is administered to aid in generation of which of the following enzymes within cells as a protective mechanism to reduce the number of free radicals?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Catalase is an enzyme found in the peroxisomes of cells that decomposes hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen . It does not directly use substrates provided by N-acetylcysteine for its action.
• Option C. Hydrogen peroxide is not an enzyme ; rather, it is a reactive oxygen species that is decomposed by enzymes such as catalase and glutathione peroxidase.
• Option D. NADPH oxidase is an enzyme complex found in phagocytes that generates reactive oxygen species , such as hydrogen peroxide, used for the killing of pathogens . It is not involved in the detoxification of free radicals related to paracetamol toxicity.
• Educational Objective:
• N-acetylcysteine aids in the synthesis of glutathione , thereby enhancing the activity of glutathione peroxidase to reduce the oxidative stress caused by a paracetamol overdose .

subject: Pathology
question: A 55-year-old man has a 30-year history of poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. He has had extensive black discoloration of skin and soft tissue of his right foot, with areas of yellowish exudate, for the past 2 months. Staphylococcus aureus is cultured from this exudate. A below-the-knee amputation is performed. The amputation specimen received in the surgical pathology laboratory is most likely to demonstrate which of the following pathologic abnormalities?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. A neoplasm is a mass lesion.
• Option C. Vasculitis (with either thrombosis or hemorrhage), would be more likely manifested throughout the body. Coagulopathy is not a feature of diabetes mellitus .
• Option D. Caseation is a part of granulomatous inflammation. Caseating granulomas are soft, cheesy, and white.
• Educational Objective:
• Gangrene, particularly wet gangrene is characterized by black discoloration and exudate in a diabetic patient, and is a consequence of coagulative necrosis coupled with bacterial infection , typically necessitating amputation of the affected area.

subject: Pathology
question: A 60-year-old man with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) presents to the hospital with shortness of breath and fever. A chest x-ray shows evidence of pneumonia, and he is started on antibiotics. Despite treatment, his condition deteriorates, and a bronchoalveolar lavage is performed, which reveals the presence of cells undergoing a type of cell death characterized by swelling, membrane rupture, and the release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs). What type of cell death is this most likely to be?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Apoptosis is a form of programmed cell death involving cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, membrane blebbing, and DNA fragmentation , without inflammation or DAMP release.
• Option B: Pyroptosis is a form of inflammatory programmed cell death associated with infection and is characterized by the activation of inflammatory caspases , cell swelling, membrane pore formation, and release of inflammatory cytokines.
• Option C: Autophagy is a process where cells degrade their own components through the lysosomal machinery , typically as a survival mechanism in response to stress, without immediate cell death or DAMP release.
• Educational Objective:
• Necroptosis is a form of programmed cell death marked by cellular swelling, membrane disruption, and DAMP release, which can be associated with conditions such as COPD and infection-related inflammation.

subject: Pathology
question: The lifestyle patterns of healthy persons from 20 to 30 years of age are studied. A subset of these persons has a lifestyle characterized by consumption of a lot of pizza and very little physical exercise. Which of the following tissue changes is most likely to develop in this subset of persons as a consequence of this lifestyle?
options: Metaplasia of muscle to adipose tissue

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fatty change in the liver is due to toxic and metabolic derangements, such as those that occur with malnutrition or alcoholism .  Similarly, fatty change in the heart (Option B) is a consequence of toxic or hypoxic events.
• Option D . Muscle does not typically undergo metaplasia in response to weight gain . Adipocytes in fascial planes and around the muscle can increase in size. The muscle may atrophy in response to the sedentary lifestyle .
• Educational Objective
• In adults, hypertrophy of adipocytes, or steatocytes, is the primary tissue change associated with weight gain due to excessive caloric intake and insufficient exercise , as this leads to an increase in fat cell size .

subject: Pathology
question: A 23-year-old woman Yashi gives birth to her first child. She begins breast feeding the infant. She continues breast feeding for almost 6 months with no difficulties and no complications. Which of the following cellular processes that began in the breast during pregnancy allowed her to nurse the infant for this period of time?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Metaplasia is the exchange of the normal epithelium for another in response to chronic irritation.
• Option B. The stroma of the breast consists of connective tissue that provides structural support , but does not have cells that produce milk.
• Option C. Dysplasia in epithelia is a pre-malignant change not part of normal physiologic events . This would be a precursor to breast cancer . Pregnancy is a protective factor against breast cancer.
• Educational Objective:
• Lobular hyperplasia that is particularly influenced by progesterone , facilitates lactation postpartum.

subject: Pathology
question: True about insulinoma is:
options: It is not associated with MEN 1 syndrome

explanation.short: Ans. C) C-peptide level is elevated
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Alpha cell tumor: This is incorrect; insulinomas arise from beta cells of the pancreas , which are responsible for the production of insulin.
• Option B: Most of them are malignant: The majority of insulinomas are benign ; only a small percentage are malignant.
• Option D: It is not associated with MEN 1 syndrome : This is incorrect; insulinomas can be associated with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 .
• Educational Objective:
• Insulinomas are beta-cell tumors , most of which are benign and can present with hypoglycemia and elevated levels of insulin and C-peptide in the blood . They may also be associated with MEN 1 syndrome .

subject: Pathology
question: Fine needle aspiration cytology is not able to detect which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Papillary carcinoma: FNAC is usually diagnostic for papillary carcinoma due to its distinctive cytological features , including nuclear grooves and psammoma bodies .
• Option B: Hashimoto thyroiditis: FNAC can suggest this diagnosis when lymphocytes and Hurthle cells are present , indicating an autoimmune process .
• Option D: Medullary cancer: FNAC can suggest medullary carcinoma by demonstrating amyloid and calcitonin in cells , but a definitive diagnosis often requires additional tests such as serum calcitonin levels.
• Educational Objective:
• While FNAC is a useful tool in the initial assessment of thyroid nodules, it cannot distinguish between follicular adenoma and follicular carcinoma due to the necessity of examining capsular and vascular invasion histologically.

subject: Pathology
question: Psammoma bodies are seen in all except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Papillary carcinoma of thyroid: It is known to frequently have psammoma bodies , which are calcified concentric structures found within the tumor tissue.
• Option C: Meningioma: This benign brain tumor often contains psammoma bodies , contributing to its classic histological appearance .
• Option D: Papillary cancer of the ovary (serous cystadenoma of the ovary): Similar to papillary thyroid carcinoma , ovarian papillary tumors can also exhibit psammoma bodies .
• Educational Objective:
• Psammoma bodies are calcified structures often found in certain neoplasms such as papillary carcinoma of the thyroid, meningioma , and papillary cancer of the ovary (serous cystadenoma of the ovary) but are not typically seen in adenocarcinoma of the colon .

subject: Pathology
question: Necrobiosis lipoidica is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Diabetes insipidus: This is a condition characterized by polyuria and polydipsia due to issues with vasopressin (ADH); it is not associated with skin changes like necrobiosis lipoidica.
• Option B: Lyme disease : Caused by Borrelia burgdorferi and transmitted by ticks , Lyme disease can present with a variety of symptoms including erythema migrans, but not necrobiosis lipoidica.
• Option D: Simmonds disease: Also known as Sheehan's syndrome , is postpartum hypopituitarism , not associated with necrobiosis lipoidica.
• Educational Objective:
• Necrobiosis lipoidica is a skin condition primarily associated with diabetes mellitus , presenting as well-circumscribed, waxy plaques typically on the shins with a yellowish center and telangiectatic borders .

subject: Pathology
question: Mauriac’s syndrome is characterized by all except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Diabetes: Mauriac's syndrome is specifically associated with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus in children .
• Option B: Obesity: Despite being a sign of poor glycemic control , obesity is not a characteristic of Mauriac's syndrome . Instead, patients may present with Cushingoid features.
• Option C: Dwarfism: Growth impairment or stunted growth is a classical component of Mauriac's syndrome due to chronic hyperglycemia and its metabolic effects.
• Educational Objective:
• Mauriac's syndrome is associated with type 1 diabetes mellitus in children and presents with hepatomegaly , growth retardation , and Cushingoid features , but not with cardiomegaly.

subject: Pathology
question: What is the primary contributor to insulin resistance in liver disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Decreased insulin release: This refers to a problem with insulin secretion from the pancreas , not resistance at the level of hepatic or peripheral tissues and does not directly relate to liver disease.
• Option C: Hepatocyte dysfunction: While hepatocyte dysfunction can contribute to metabolic disturbances , it is a broader term that includes various liver impairments, not specific to insulin resistance.
• Option D: Decreased ‘C’ peptide level: C-peptide is a byproduct of insulin production . Lower levels may indicate reduced insulin secretion but are not a direct cause of insulin resistance in the liver.
• Educational Objective:
• Hepatic steatosis , or fatty liver disease , is a significant contributor to insulin resistance , which impairs both the liver's response to insulin and the peripheral action of insulin .

subject: Pathology
question: According to ADA guidelines, the diagnosis of diabetes is made when the fasting blood glucose is more than?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: 100 mg/dL: This is considered the upper limit of normal for fasting glucose , with levels between 100 mg/dL and 125 mg/dL indicating impaired fasting glucose or prediabetes.
• Option C: 140 mg/dL: This level is above the diagnostic criteria for diabetes when measured fasting and may also indicate diabetes if it is a 2-hour postprandial or oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) value.
• Option D: 200 mg/dL: A level of 200 mg/dL or higher may indicate diabetes if it is from a random plasma glucose test accompanied by classic symptoms of hyperglycemia or hyperglycemic crisis, or during an OGTT.
• Educational Objective:
• A fasting blood glucose level of 126 mg/dL or higher is diagnostic for diabetes mellitus according to ADA guidelines .

subject: Pathology
question: Most common association in MEN I is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Insulinoma: Insulinomas are also associated with MEN I and result in hypoglycemia due to unregulated insulin secretion , but they are less common compared to gastrinomas.
• Option C: Lipoma : While patients with MEN I may develop non-endocrine tumors like lipomas , these are not as commonly associated with the syndrome as endocrine tumors.
• Option D: Glucagonoma : Glucagonomas, which secrete glucagon , are rare pancreatic endocrine tumors and are less commonly associated with MEN I than gastrinomas.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of MEN I , gastrinomas are the most commonly associated functional pancreatic endocrine tumors , often leading to Zollinger-Ellison syndrome , characterized by peptic ulcer disease , gastroesophageal reflux , and diarrhea due to hypergastrinemia .

subject: Pathology
question: What marker is indicative of a non-functional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (PNET)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: CD100: CD100 is not a recognized marker for PNETs . CD markers are generally used in the characterization of lymphocytes and other cells of the immune system.
• Option C: CEA: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a tumor marker associated with colorectal cancer and some other adenocarcinomas, not specifically with PNETs.
• Option D: PSA: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is used as a marker for prostate cancer . It has no relevance in the diagnosis of PNETs.
• Educational Objective:
• Chromogranin A is a marker for pancreatic non-functional neuroendocrine tumours .

subject: Pathology
question: A 62-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral lower extremity swelling and fatigue for the past several months. He has a past medical history significant for poorly controlled diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated serum creatinine and proteinuria. A renal biopsy is performed, which demonstrates deposition of amyloid proteins in the glomeruli. Which of the following types of diabetes mellitus is most commonly associated with this renal complication?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Maturity onset diabetes of the young (MODY) is characterized by monogenic defects of β-cell function but is not typically associated with amyloid deposition in the islets.
• Option B: Type I DM is an autoimmune disorder leading to β-cell destruction and does not typically involve amyloid deposition.
• Educational Objective:
• Amyloid deposition in the islets of Langerhans is a feature specifically associated with Type II diabetes mellitus , which can contribute to the dysfunction of insulin-secreting pancreatic β-cells .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following thyroid carcinoma variants is not associated with aggressiveness?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Unencapsulated: Lack of encapsulation is a feature associated with increased aggressiveness in papillary carcinoma . Encapsulation can help contain the tumor and prevent its spread . So, this option matches the criteria for aggressiveness.
• Option C: Tall cell variant: The tall cell variant of papillary carcinoma is associated with a more aggressive behavior compared to classical papillary carcinoma . Therefore, this option matches the criteria for aggressiveness.
• Option D: Oxyphilic (Hurthle) cell type: Hurthle cell carcinoma, a variant of follicular thyroid carcinoma , is generally considered more aggressive than papillary carcinoma.
• Educational Objective:
• The f ollicular variant of papillary carcinoma tends to have a better prognosis compared to classical papillary carcinoma as it is less aggressive in its behavior .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following thyroid carcinomas originates from C-cells?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Follicular carcinoma: This carcinoma arises from follicular cells , which are responsible for synthesizing and secreting thyroid hormones , not calcitonin.
• Option C: Papillary carcinoma: The most common type of thyroid cancer , also derived from follicular cells , and not from C-cells.
• Option D: Anaplastic carcinoma: A rare and highly aggressive thyroid cancer that arises from follicular cells and has a poor prognosis ; it does not originate from C-cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Medullary carcinoma of the thyroid is derived from C-cells , which are distinct from follicular cells that give rise to the more common papillary and follicular thyroid carcinomas . Medullary thyroid carcinoma is characterized by the production of calcitonin and may be associated with multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2).

subject: Pathology
question: Hurthle cell tumor is:
options: Ionizing radiation in early decades is a major risk factor

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Papillary carcinoma – This is the most common type of thyroid carcinoma , but Hurthle cell tumors are not classified as papillary carcinoma.
• Option C: Medullary carcinoma – This type arises from parafollicular C cells , which is not related to the follicular cells that give rise to Hurthle cell tumors .
• Option D: Ionizing radiation in early decades is a major risk factor – While exposure to ionizing radiation is a known risk factor for thyroid carcinoma , it is not specifically tied to the pathogenesis of Hurthle cell tumors, which are a variant of follicular carcinoma.
• Educational Objective:
• Hurthle cell tumors are a variant of follicular thyroid carcinoma , which are characterized by distinctive large cells with abundant granular cytoplasm and are encapsulated . They differ from papillary and medullary thyroid carcinomas both in terms of their origin and histological appearance.

subject: Pathology
question: About papillary carcinoma of thyroid, all are true except:

explanation.short: Ans. C) Early metastasis with poor prognosis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Prognosis better: This is true . Papillary carcinoma of the thyroid generally has an excellent prognosis, especially when diagnosed early and in younger patients.
• Option B: Psammoma body present in 50% cases: Psammoma bodies are calcifications that are often found in papillary thyroid carcinoma but may not be present in as many as 50% of cases.
• Option D: Spreads by the lymphatic route: This is true . Papillary thyroid carcinoma commonly spreads to regional lymph nodes via lymphatics.
• Educational Objective:
• Papillary carcinoma of the thyroid typically has a favorable prognosis , even with early lymphatic spread , contrasting with other cancers where early metastasis often correlates with a poor prognosis .

subject: Pathology
question: A 51-year-old man Sonu with a history of recurrent calcium-containing renal stones presents to the emergency room with excruciating flank pain and blood in the urine. This patient is likely to have which of the following underlying disorders?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Anemia of chronic disease – This condition is associated with long-standing , chronic illnesses that lead to alterations in iron metabolism and erythropoiesis but is not directly related to calcium stone formation.
• Option B: Chronic Proteus infection – While Proteus bacteria can increase the risk for struvite stone formation due to urease production , it is not typically associated with calcium stone formation.
• Option D: Hyperaldosteronism – This condition leads to hypertension and hypokalemia due to excess aldosterone and is not typically associated with calcium-containing renal stone disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Recurrent calcium-containing renal stones may suggest an underlying disorder of calcium metabolism, such as hyperparathyroidism , due to its association with increased PTH levels , hypercalcemia , and hypercalciuria .

subject: Pathology
question: Most common thyroid cancer after radiation exposure is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Medullary cancer: Medullary thyroid cancer arises from parafollicular C-cells and is typically associated with genetic mutations in the RET proto-oncogene , not with radiation exposure.
• Option C: Follicular cancer: While not associated with radiation exposure , the incidence of follicular carcinoma is higher in areas with iodine deficiency.
• Option D: Anaplastic cancer: Anaplastic thyroid cancer is a rare and aggressive form of thyroid cancer that generally occurs in the elderly population and is not associated with radiation exposure.
• Educational Objective:
• Papillary thyroid carcinoma is the most common thyroid cancer associated with childhood exposure to ionizing radiation , which can significantly increase the risk of this cancer .

subject: Pathology
question: Orphan Annie eye nuclei appearance is characteristic of:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei is a distinctive cytological feature of papillary carcinoma of the thyroid , which is important for diagnosis and distinguishing it from other neoplasms.

subject: Pathology
question: The laboratory screening test which suggests normal thyroid function is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Free T4: Free T4 measurement reflects the biologically active portion of thyroxine and can be normal in early or mild forms of thyroid dysfunction .
• Option C: T3: Total T3 levels can be affected by factors that influence binding proteins and are not the first line of screening; they are more helpful in diagnosing hyperthyroidism or in certain clinical situations like T3 toxicosis.
• Option D: Free T3: Similar to T3, Free T3 is not the first line of screening but rather used for specific clinical indications and is less commonly measured than TSH or Free T4.
• Educational Objective:
• TSH is the most sensitive initial screening test for evaluating thyroid function because it reflects the pituitary's response to the serum concentration of free thyroid hormones , particularly free T4.

subject: Pathology
question: Medullary carcinoma of the thyroid is associated with which of the following syndrome:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: MEN I – Incorrect. MEN I typically involves tumors of the parathyroid glands , pituitary gland , and pancreas , not medullary thyroid carcinoma.
• Option C: Li-Fraumeni syndrome – Incorrect. This is a hereditary cancer syndrome associated with a variety of cancers , including sarcomas, breast cancer, and brain tumors, but not specifically medullary thyroid carcinoma.
• Option D: Hashimoto’s thyroiditis – Incorrect. This is an autoimmune disease of the thyroid gland and is not directly associated with medullary thyroid carcinoma , although thyroid cancer can occur more frequently in the setting of Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
• Educational Objective:
• Medullary thyroid carcinoma is commonly associated with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type II (MEN II), which includes two subtypes , MEN IIA and MEN IIB , both characterized by the occurrence of medullary thyroid carcinoma and p heochromocytomas . MEN IIA also includes parathyroid adenomas or hyperplasia.

subject: Pathology
question: A 55-year-old woman with a family history of endocrine neoplasia presents with a thyroid nodule. Histological examination of the nodule after fine-needle aspiration biopsy reveals amyloid deposition. Which of the following types of thyroid carcinoma is the woman most likely to have?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Papillary carcinoma: This is the most common type of thyroid cancer and is characterized by branching papillae and often presents with psammoma bodies . It is not typically associated with amyloid deposition.
• Option B: Follicular carcinoma : It does not characteristically involve amyloid deposition .
• Option D: Anaplastic carcinoma: This is a highly aggressive and undifferentiated form of thyroid cancer . Amyloid is not a feature of anaplastic thyroid carcinoma.
• Educational Objective:
• Medullary thyroid carcinoma is unique among thyroid cancers as it originates from parafollicular C-cells and is associated with amyloid deposition within the tumor , which can be identified histologically. This type of carcinoma can occur sporadically or as part of the multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 syndromes.

subject: Pathology
question: Which is not seen in MEN I:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type I (MEN I) is characterized by the presence of tumors in parathyroid glands , anterior pituitary, and pancreatic islets , and does not commonly include medullary carcinoma of the thyroid, which is associated with MEN II.

subject: Pathology
question: A 47-year-old woman presents with a solitary thyroid nodule. Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) is performed, and the pathologist reports that the distinction between a benign and a malignant lesion cannot be made with certainty. Which type of thyroid nodule is most notorious for requiring histologic examination beyond FNAC to establish a definitive diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Follicular carcinoma of thyroid
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Papillary carcinoma of thyroid : Papillary carcinoma typically shows distinctive nuclear features on FNAC which are usually diagnostic, making it sufficient for diagnosis.
• Option B: Carcinoma breast : FNAC can be diagnostic for breast carcinoma, showing malignant cells , but a core biopsy is often preferred for additional architectural and immunohistochemical information.
• Option C: Adenocarcinoma lung : While FNAC can suggest adenocarcinoma of the lung by demonstrating malignant glandular cells, a core biopsy is usually required for complete characterization.
• Educational Objective:
• While FNAC can suggest follicular neoplasm , it cannot differentiate follicular carcinoma from follicular adenoma as the latter requires the identification of capsular or vascular invasion, which necessitates histological examination of the entire surgical specimen.

subject: Pathology
question: A 56-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with a history of a progressively enlarging neck mass over the past several years. She reports no symptoms of dysphagia, dyspnea, or hoarseness. Physical examination reveals a palpable, non-tender, firm mass in the anterior neck consistent with a long-standing goitre. Which malignancy develops in long standing goitre?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Papillary carcinoma: This is the most common thyroid malignancy and can occur with or without a preceding goiter . It has a distinctive histological appearance but is not particularly associated with iodine deficiency or long-standing goiters.
• Option C: Medullary carcinoma: This type of cancer arises from parafollicular C cells producing calcitonin and is commonly associated with genetic syndromes like MEN 2, rather than long-standing goiters.
• Option D: Anaplastic carcinoma: This is a very aggressive form of thyroid cancer that can arise de novo or from existing well-differentiated tumors but isn't specifically linked to long-standing goiters.
• Educational Objective:
• Follicular carcinoma of the thyroid is associated with iodine deficiency and is more likely to develop in the setting of a long-standing goiter . This malignancy is notorious for hematogenous spread and requires thorough histological examination to differentiate it from benign conditions.

subject: Pathology
question: A 40-year-old patient presents with symptoms of hypercalcemia, peptic ulcer disease, and a pituitary mass on imaging. Given these clinical features, which of the following conditions is least likely to be part of the patient's underlying syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Foregut carcinoid - Foregut carcinoids (including those found in the thymus, lung, and stomach) are part of the spectrum of MEN-1 syndrome , although less common compared to other manifestations.
• Option C: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors - These tumors are a hallmark of MEN-1 and can produce a variety of symptoms depending on the hormone they secrete (e.g., gastrin, insulin).
• Option D: Parathyroid hyperplasia - Primary hyperparathyroidism due to parathyroid hyperplasia is the most common feature of MEN-1 syndrome, causing hypercalcemia.
• Educational Objective:
• MEN-1 syndrome, also known as Wermer syndrome, is characterized by anterior pituitary tumors, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors , and parathyroid hyperplasia, but not by tumors of the posterior pituitary.

subject: Pathology
question: All of the following are feature of granulomatous thyroiditis except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: In the initial phase of granulomatous (subacute) thyroiditis , there may be a transient hyperthyroid phase due to the inflammatory destruction of thyroid follicles leading to the release of preformed thyroid hormones into circulation .
• Option B: Following the hyperthyroid phase of granulomatous thyroiditis, there is a hypothyroid phase as the damaged thyroid may be temporarily unable to produce sufficient hormones . This is usually self-limited, and normal thyroid function often returns, but some patients may develop permanent hypothyroidism.
• Option D: Granulomatous thyroiditis is characterized histologically by granuloma formation with giant cells . These multinucleated giant cells are part of the body's response to inflammation and are involved in the degradation of thyroglobulin, which is released during the follicular destruction.
• Educational Objective:
• Granulomatous thyroiditis is the most common cause of thyroid pain, not a painless condition.

subject: Pathology
question: Brown tumour is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Brown tumors are associated with hyperparathyroidism and represent a severe manifestation of osteitis fibrosa cystica , where excessive bone resorption leads to fibrous and cystic bone changes .

subject: Pathology
question: Most common cause of Cushing’s syndrome is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: A pituitary adenoma that secretes adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) can cause Cushing's disease , which is a subset of Cushing's syndrome. This is the most common cause of endogenous Cushing's syndrome , but overall less common than the exogenous form caused by taking corticosteroid medications.
• Option C: An adrenal adenoma is a benign tumor of the adrenal gland that can autonomously secrete cortisol . It's a less common cause of Cushing's syndrome compared to exogenous steroid use and pituitary tumors but should be considered, especially when ACTH levels are low.
• Option D: Adrenal carcinomas are rare but can also cause Cushing's syndrome by secreting excessive amounts of cortisol . They are more aggressive than adenomas and can be associated with a worse prognosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Exogenous corticosteroids are the most common cause of Cushing's syndrome , primarily due to the widespread use of corticosteroid medications for a variety of medical conditions.

subject: Pathology
question: Submucosal neuroma is associated with:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type I (MEN I) is a syndrome characterized by the presence of tumors in the parathyroid glands , pancreatic islet cells , and the anterior pituitary gland . Submucosal neuromas are not typically associated with MEN I.
• Option B: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type II A (MEN II A) is a syndrome that includes medullary thyroid carcinoma , pheochromocytoma , and primary hyperparathyroidism . It does not typically present with submucosal neuromas.
• Educational Objective:
• Submucosal neuromas are associated with MEN II B and can contribute to the diagnosis of this syndrome.

subject: Pathology
question: Most common site of pheochromocytoma after adrenal gland is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: The hilum of the kidney is an area where the renal artery enters and the renal vein and ureter exit . While it is close to the adrenal glands , it is not a common site for extra-adrenal pheochromocytomas , which are tumors that arise from chromaffin cells typically found in the adrenal medulla.
• Option C: While chromaffin tissue can be found along the sympathetic chain in the neck , pheochromocytomas are not commonly found in this location.
• Option D: Pheochromocytomas can occur in the urinary bladder , but it is an uncommon site . When they do occur in this location, they can cause symptoms such as hypertension, palpitations, and headache during micturition.
• Educational Objective:
• Organs of Zuckerkandl are the most common extra-adrenal pheochromocytoma .

subject: Pathology
question: True about adrenal pheochromocytoma is:

explanation.short: Ans. C) Bilateral in 10% of cases
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Adrenal pheochromocytomas are typically chromaffin positive , meaning they stain positively for chromaffin , which is indicative of catecholamine-secreting cells . These tumors arise from chromaffin cells in the adrenal medulla.
• Option B: While pheochromocytomas can be malignant , most are benign . The malignancy rate is estimated to be about 10% in adrenal pheochromocytomas. Malignancy is usually determined by the presence of metastasis rather than histological features.
• Option D: This statement is incorrect because unilateral pheochromocytomas are much more common than 10%. The majority of pheochromocytomas are unilateral.
• Educational Objective:
• While most adrenal pheochromocytomas are benign and unilateral, they can be bilateral in 10% of cases , especially in hereditary syndromes .

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old male presents to the emergency department with complaints of episodic headaches, palpitations, and sweating. Physical examination reveals elevated blood pressure and a palpable abdominal mass. Which of the following tumors is most likely to exhibit the "rule of 10" characteristic?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Oncocytomas are benign renal epithelial tumors that typically do not follow the "rule of 10s". They are usually asymptomatic and are often found incidentally during imaging for other conditions.
• Option C: Lymphomas are cancers of the lymphatic system and do not follow the "rule of 10s". They have various subtypes with differing presentations and prognoses.
• Option D: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common type of kidney cancer in adults and does not adhere to the "rule of 10s". RCC has different subtypes with varying clinical behaviors and genetic associations.
• Educational Objective:
• Pheochromocytoma is classically described by the "rule of 10s", which can be helpful in remembering the characteristics of this tumor for diagnosis and management decisions .

subject: Pathology
question: Which one of the following is not seen in pheochromocytoma?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hypertension is a hallmark sign of pheochromocytoma and can be sustained or paroxysmal due to episodic catecholamine secretion by the tumor.
• Option B: Episodic palpitations are a common symptom of pheochromocytoma due to episodic release of catecholamines , particularly norepinephrine , which stimulates the heart and causes increased contractility and heart rate.
• Option C: Weight loss is frequently seen in pheochromocytoma patients . The excess catecholamines can increase metabolism and cause a hyperadrenergic state , leading to weight loss even in the presence of a good appetite.
• Educational Objective:
• While hypertension , episodic palpitations , and weight loss are common in pheochromocytoma , diarrhea is not typically associated with this condition.

subject: Pathology
question: Mitotic figures and giant cells are seen in which of the following tumor?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Benign and malignant pheochromocytoma
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Thyroid cancers can indeed present with mitotic figures , especially in more aggressive forms such as anaplastic thyroid carcinoma . However, giant cells are particularly characteristic of certain inflammatory conditions such as subacute thyroiditis, rather than thyroid neoplasms.
• Option C: Benign and malignant liver tumors \ such as hepatocellular carcinoma can show mitotic figures , indicating a high proliferative rate . However, giant cells are more typical of specific liver pathologies such as giant cell hepatitis, not necessarily of benign or malignant liver tumors.
• Option D: In renal tumors , mitotic figures are also indicative of a higher grade of malignancy , especially in clear cell renal carcinoma . Giant cells are not a characteristic feature of common benign or malignant renal tumors.
• Educational Objective:
• The histological presence of mitotic figures and giant cells in pheochromocytomas is not indicative of malignancy ; instead, malignancy is confirmed by the presence of metastatic disease .

subject: Pathology
question: Homer wright rosettes are seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Also known as Wilms' tumor , nephroblastoma is a kidney tumor usually found in children . It can show other types of rosette formations, but not typically Homer Wright rosettes.
• Option C: Hepatoma , or hepatocellular carcinoma , is a liver tumor and does not feature the presence of Homer Wright rosettes. Its pathology is markedly different from that of neuroblastic tumors.
• Option D: Ependymomas are glial cell tumors that can form rosettes known as perivascular rosettes or ependymal rosettes , which are distinct from Homer Wright rosettes.
• Educational Objective:
• Homer wright rosettes are seen in Neuroblastoma.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not part of the “rule of 10” in pheochromocytoma?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: In the context of pheochromocytoma , approximately 10% of tumors can be found bilaterally , often associated with hereditary conditions such as Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia types 2A and 2B , von Hippel-Lindau disease , and neurofibromatosis type 1 .
• Option B: Pheochromocytomas are usually benign , but about 10% can be malignant . Malignancy is determined by the presence of metastases rather than the histological appearance of the primary tumor.
• Option C: Around 10% of pheochromocytomas are located outside of the adrenal glands , commonly in the abdomen in locations like the organ of Zuckerkandl . These are termed paragangliomas.
• Educational Objective:
• The "rule of 10" in pheochromocytoma refers to the observation that approximately:
• 10% of pheochromocytomas are extra-adrenal. 10% of pheochromocytomas are bilateral, affecting both adrenal glands. 10% of pheochromocytomas are malignant.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not a feature of Sipple Syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Pheochromocytoma is a characteristic feature of MEN 2A and presents as a catecholamine-secreting tumor typically originating in the adrenal medulla.
• Option B: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) is a neuroendocrine tumor of the thyroid and a principal feature of MEN 2A . It arises from the parafollicular C-cells of the thyroid gland and secretes calcitonin.
• Option D: Primary hyperparathyroidism is part of the MEN 2A syndrome and is due to hyperplasia or adenoma of the parathyroid glands leading to excessive secretion of parathyroid hormone (PTH).
• Educational Objective:
• While pheochromocytoma , medullary thyroid carcinoma , and hyperparathyroidism are part of the Sipple syndrome , hyperthyroidism is not.

subject: Pathology
question: Mechanism of action of an aprepitant is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. RANK ligand inhibitor: Incorrect because RANK ligand inhibitors like denosumab are used to treat osteoporosis and bone metastases , not nausea and vomiting. These drugs inhibit RANK ligand, a protein essential for the formation and survival of osteoclasts, which are involved in bone resorption.
• Option B. NMDA antagonist: Incorrect as NMDA antagonists, such as memantine , target cognitive decline in Alzheimer’s disease rather than nausea and vomiting.
• Option D. 5 HT3 antagonist: Incorrect because 5-HT3 antagonists, like ondansetron and granisetron , also prevent nausea and vomiting in settings like chemotherapy and post-operation but function by blocking 5-HT3 receptors on the vagus nerve and in the central nervous system , not NK1 receptors.
• Educational Objective:
• NK1 receptor antagonist used primarily to prevent delayed chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting by inhibiting the action of substance P on NK1 receptors.

subject: Pathology
question: Despite their short half-lives (2 hrs), proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) cause a prolonged suppression of acid secretion (up to 48 hrs) because?
options: They are pro-drugs and undergo activation gradually
From stomach, these quickly reach the parietal cells and enter acid secretory canaliculi and stay there, blocking the secretion of acid for a long time
They irreversible inhibit the proton pump molecule
They are available as enteric coated capsules, from which drug is gradually released

explanation.short: Ans. C) They irreversible inhibit the proton pump molecule
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. They are pro-drugs and undergo activation gradually: Incorrect because while PPIs are indeed prodrugs that are activated within the acidic environment of the parietal cells , the gradual activation does not directly contribute to the prolonged acid suppression . The prolongation comes from the irreversible inhibition of the pumps.
• Option B. From stomach, these quickly reach the parietal cells and enter acid secretory canaliculi and stay there, blocking the secretion of acid for a long time: Incorrect because PPIs do not travel directly from the stomach to the parietal cells. They must first be absorbed from the intestine and then distributed to the parietal cells via the bloodstream.
• Option D. They are available as enteric coated capsules, from which drug is gradually released: Incorrect as the enteric coating only serves to protect the drug from gastric acid degradation rather than prolonging its action at the site of the proton pumps.
• Educational Objective:
• Proton pump inhibitor s cause prolonged acid suppression by irreversibly binding to and inactivating the proton pumps in the stomach, and acid secretion only resumes once new pumps are synthesized and active.

subject: Pathology
question: A patient of peptic ulcer was prescribed ranitidine and sucralfate in the morning hours. Why is this combination incorrect?
options: Ranitidine combines with sucralfate and prevents its action
Combination of these two drugs produces serious side effects like agranulocytosis
Ranitidine increases the gastric pH so sucralfate is not able to act
Sucralfate inhibits the absorption of ranitidine

explanation.short: Ans. C) Ranitidine increases the gastric pH so sucralfate is not able to act
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ranitidine combines with sucralfate and prevents its action: Incorrect because ranitidine does not chemically combine with sucralfate ; the issue is the pH environment alteration.
• Option B. Combination of these two drugs produces serious side effects like agranulocytosis: Incorrect as there is no evidence to suggest that agranulocytosis is a common side effect of combining these two drugs.
• Option D. Sucralfate inhibits the absorption of ranitidine: Incorrect because sucralfate does not affect the absorption of ranitidine; its primary interaction is with the gastric pH affecting its own mechanism of action.
• Educational Objective:
• Sucralfate needs an acidic environment to be effective. Co-administration with ranitidine, which raises gastric pH, can impair the effectiveness of sucralfate in treating ulcers.

subject: Pathology
question: All of the following adverse effects are associated with the use of proton pump inhibitors except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Community acquired pneumonia: Correctly associated with PPIs. Some studies have indicated an increased risk of pneumonia, particularly in vulnerable groups such as the elderly or those with existing respiratory conditions , possibly due to alterations in stomach pH affecting gut and respiratory flora.
• Option B. Clostridium difficile infection: Correctly associated with PPIs. The reduction in gastric acidity can lead to an environment more conducive to the growth of Clostridium difficile , potentially resulting in severe and life-threatening infections.
• Option C. Osteoporosis: Correctly associated with PPIs . Chronic use of PPIs may impact calcium absorption and bone metabolism, leading to an increased risk of osteoporosis and related fractures, especially in populations at risk like postmenopausal women and the elderly.
• Educational Objective:
• While PPIs are linked to several significant adverse effects such as increased risks of community-acquired pneumonia, Clostridium difficile infections , and osteoporosis , they are not associated with causing or worsening hypothyroidism.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following drugs have least affinity for CYP3A4?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Omeprazole: Incorrect because Omeprazole is extensively metabolized by CYP3A4, making it more likely to interact with other drugs metabolized by this enzyme.
• Option B. Lansoprazole: Incorrect as Lansoprazole also has significant metabolism via CYP3A4, similar to omeprazole, which increases its potential for drug interactions.
• Option C. Esomeprazole: Incorrect because Esomeprazole, the S-isomer of omeprazole, is extensively metabolized by CYP3A4 , thus having a higher affinity for this enzyme compared to rabeprazole.
• Educational Objective:
• Rabeprazole has the least affinity for the enzyme CYP3A4 among the listed PPIs , potentially leading to fewer drug interactions related to metabolism by this pathway.

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old pregnant woman has a history of rheumatoid arthritis which has been managed successfully with NSAIDs. However, she has recently visited her general practitioner complaining of burning epigastric pain worsened by food intake. Which of the following ulcer medication is most likely contraindicated in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Famotidine: Incorrect as Famotidine, an H2 receptor antagonist, is generally considered safe for use during pregnancy . It works by reducing stomach acid and is not known to harm the fetus.
• Option B. Omeprazole: Incorrect because Omeprazole , a proton pump inhibitor , is also used to manage ulcer symptoms by reducing acid production. While its use in pregnancy should be cautious, it is not specifically contraindicated like Misoprostol.
• Option D. Ranitidine: Incorrect as Ranitidine, another H2 receptor antagonist, is typically safe for use during pregnancy. It works similarly to Famotidine by reducing gastric acid and is not associated with significant risks to the developing fetus.
• Educational Objective:
• Misoprostol is contraindicated in pregnant patients due to its potential to induce uterine contractions and risk causing abortion.

subject: Pathology
question: Drug implicated in prolonging QT interval is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Domperidone: Incorrect because while Domperidone does have some cardiac risk , it is more specifically associated with an increased risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death rather than QT prolongation.
• Option B. Metoclopramide: Incorrect as Metoclopramide, another prokinetic agent, primarily affects dopamine receptors and has not been commonly associated with QT prolongation.
• Option D. Omeprazole: Incorrect because Omeprazole, a proton pump inhibitor, does not typically cause QT prolongation as part of its side effect profile.
• Educational Objective:
• Cisapride is associated with QT interval prolongation and can lead to torsades de pointes , particularly at high concentrations or when combined with other drugs that inhibit its metabolism.
• Important drugs causing QT prolongation

subject: Pathology
question: All the following statements about treatment of diarrhea are correct except?
options: Opioids delay passage of gut contents by reducing peristalsis
Loperamide is an opioid with anti-motility action
Anti-motility drugs are best drugs for infective diarrhea
Diphenoxylate overdose can cause respiratory depression

explanation.short: Ans. C) Anti-motility drugs are best drugs for infective diarrhea
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Opioids delay passage of gut contents by reducing peristalsis: Correct , opioids inhibit gastrointestinal motility, leading to slower transit times and more water absorption , which can help alleviate diarrhea symptoms.
• Option B. Loperamide is an opioid with anti-motility action: Correct , loperamide acts on opioid receptors in the gastrointestinal tract to reduce motility and increase the absorption of fluids, making it effective in managing diarrhea.
• Option D. Diphenoxylate overdose can cause respiratory depression: Correct, diphenoxylate is an opioid and, like other opioids, can cause significant respiratory depression if overdosed.
• Educational Objective:
• While anti-motility drugs are effective in managing non-infective diarrhea, they are not recommended for treating infective diarrhea due to the risk of prolonging the presence of pathogens in the gastrointestinal tract.

subject: Pathology
question: False statement about racecadotril is?
options: It is a pro-drug that is converted to peripherally acting enkephalinase inhibitor
It is a drug used in treatment of constipation
It has anti- secretory action in GIT

explanation.short: Ans. B) It is drug used in constipation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It is a pro-drug that is converted to peripherally acting enkephalinase inhibitor: Correct , racecadotril is a pro-drug that is metabolized into thiorphan , which acts peripherally to inhibit enkephalinase , thus prolonging the action of naturally occurring opioids that decrease gut motility.
• Option C. It is metabolized by liver: Correct, racecadotril undergoes metabolic processes in the liver, converting it into its active form.
• Option D. It has anti-secretory action in GIT: Correct, by inhibiting the breakdown of enkephalins, racecadotril enhances their natural anti-secretory effects in the gastrointestinal tract, which is beneficial in treating diarrhea.
• Educational Objective:
• Racecadotril is indicated for the treatment of acute diarrhea and works by reducing gastrointestinal secretion; it is not used for constipation and indeed would be contraindicated in such cases.

subject: Pathology
question: Racecadotril is used for?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chronic diarrhea: Incorrect because Racecadotril is not used for chronic diarrhea conditions as its effects are more suited to acute cases.
• Option C. Chronic constipation: Incorrect as Racecadotril's mechanism of reducing fluid secretion would be detrimental in constipation , potentially worsening the condition.
• Option D. Diabetic gastroparesis: Incorrect because Racecadotril does not affect gastric emptying or motility , which are the primary concerns in diabetic gastroparesis.
• Educational Objective:
• Racecadotril is specifically used for acute secretory diarrhea due to its ability to reduce fluid secretion in the gut , thereby decreasing stool output without significantly affecting intestinal motility.

subject: Pathology
question: Most important mechanism of action for antiemetic property of metoclopramide is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 5-HT3 antagonist: Incorrect because while 5-HT3 antagonists are indeed used as antiemetics, metoclopramide does not act through this mechanism. Drugs like ondansetron are typical examples of 5-HT3 antagonists.
• Option C. 5-HT4 agonist: Incorrect as metoclopramide does have some agonistic action at 5-HT4 receptors which promotes gastric emptying, but this is not the primary mechanism responsible for its antiemetic effects.
• Option D. M3-antagonist: Incorrect because metoclopramide does not function as an M3 muscarinic antagonist . Antimuscarinics like scopolamine work by this mechanism for their antiemetic effects, which is different from how metoclopramide operates.
• Educational Objective

subject: Pathology
question: You are going on a trip to a hill station with a friend who developed nausea and vomiting while travelling in the hills previously. Which of the following measure can be taken to prevent such an episode this time?
options: Diphenhydramine one hour before the trip
Scopolamine patch one night before the trip
Ranitidine one night and one hour before the trip

explanation.short: Ans. B) Scopolamine patch one night before the trip
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Diphenhydramine one hour before the trip: Incorrect as diphenhydramine, while also used to treat motion sickness due to its antihistaminic properties, is generally considered less effective than scopolamine. It also has sedative effects , which can be undesirable for some travelers.
• Option C. Ranitidine one night and one hour before the trip: Incorrect because Ranitidine is an H2 receptor antagonist primarily used for reducing gastric acid and does not have antiemetic properties effective against motion sickness.
• Option D. Pantoprazole one hour before the trip: Incorrect as Pantoprazole , a proton pump inhibitor, reduces stomach acid production but does not prevent nausea and vomiting due to motion sickness.
• Educational Objective:
• Scopolamine patch is a very effective preventive measure against motion sickness due to its muscarinic antagonist properties and should be applied 12 hours before travel to ensure efficacy.

subject: Pathology
question: On a trip, 4 hours after having lunch at a restaurant, your friend develops diarrhea, and had 5 loose stools. There are signs of minimal dehydration. What should be your management?
options: Buy ORS and give after preparing according to instructions.
ORS and antibiotics, both tinidazole and ofloxacin.
Give only ofloxacin because tinidazole is not required as Giardia will not cause diarrhea within 4 hours.
Go to a hospital as he needs to be shown immediately, as night is approaching.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Buy ORS and give after preparing according to instructions
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. ORS and antibiotics, both tinidazole and ofloxacin: Incorrect because antibiotics are not recommended without a specific diagnosis that indicates a bacterial infection, and their unnecessary use can lead to resistance and other complications. Furthermore, antibiotics are not effective against viruses or non-bacterial agents that might cause diarrhea.
• Option C. Give only ofloxacin because tinidazole is not required as Giardia will not cause diarrhea within 4 hours: Incorrect as this assumes a specific diagnosis without evidence , and the use of antibiotics like ofloxacin should be guided by a medical professional following appropriate tests.
• Option D. Go to a hospital as he needs to be shown immediately, as night is approaching: Not necessary at this stage as the symptoms are mild and manageable with ORS. Hospital visits are advised if symptoms escalate or if there are signs of severe dehydration or other complications .
• Educational Objective:
• ORS is the first line of management for mild cases of diarrhea with minimal dehydration . It's effective, safe, and does not contribute to antibiotic resistance.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is a Guanylate Cyclase C agonist used in irritable bowel syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lubiprostone: Incorrect because Lubiprostone works by activating chloride channels rather than acting as a guanylate Cyclase-C agonist. It is used primarily for treating chronic idiopathic constipation and IBS-C but via a different mechanism.
• Option C. Tegaserod: Incorrect as Tegaserod is a 5-HT4 receptor agonist, not a guanylate Cyclase-C agonist. It’s used to treat IBS-C in women by enhancing peristaltic reflex and bowel motility.
• Option D. Methyl naltrexone: Incorrect because Methyl naltrexone is a peripheral opioid receptor antagonist used specifically for opioid-induced constipation , acting by blocking the effects of opioids in the gut without affecting the central nervous system.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Pathology
question: Lubiprostone is a?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Chloride channel inhibitor: Incorrect as Lubiprostone does the opposite by activating, not inhibiting, chloride channels.
• Option C. Sodium channel activator: Incorrect because Lubiprostone does not affect sodium channels ; its action is specific to chloride channels.
• Option D. Sodium channel inhibitor: Incorrect as Lubiprostone has no activity on sodium channels , either as an activator or inhibitor.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is an antagonist of a peptide and is used to reduce chemotherapy induced nausea and vomiting?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Atrial natriuretic peptide: Incorrect as this peptide is involved in the regulation of blood pressure and fluid balance, not in the treatment of nausea or vomiting.
• Option C. Bradykinin: Incorrect because Bradykinin is a peptide that plays a role in inflammation and blood pressure regulation , not directly in the control of nausea or vomiting.
• Option D. Enalapril: Incorrect as Enalapril is an ACE inhibitor used primarily to treat high blood pressure and heart failure, and has no role in managing chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.
• Educational Objective:
• Aprepitant is an NK1 receptor antagonist used specifically to prevent chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting by blocking the action of substance P.

subject: Pathology
question: Drug used in irritable bowel syndrome with constipation is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Loperamide: Incorrect because Loperamide is primarily an anti-diarrheal medication, used to treat irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea (IBS-D) due to its action as a µ-opioid receptor agonist, which decreases intestinal motility.
• Option C. Alosetron: Incorrect as Alosetron is a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist used specifically for IBS with predominant diarrhea (IBS-D), where it helps by reducing visceral pain and slowing colonic transit.
• Option D. Clonidine: Incorrect because Clonidine is an alpha-2 agonist used mainly in the management of hypertension and not typically for BSI . It has some applications in diabetic diarrhea , but not in IBS with constipation.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Pathology
question: Which is not an osmotic laxative?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sorbitol: Incorrect as Sorbitol is an osmotic laxative that works by drawing water into the large intestine, increasing the water content in stools , which makes them easier to pass.
• Option B. Magnesium (e.g., magnesium citrate): Incorrect because magnesium compounds are osmotic laxatives . They increase the amount of water in the intestines , primarily through osmosis, which softens the stool and naturally stimulates bowel movement.
• Option C. Polyethylene glycol: Incorrect as it is an osmotic laxative known for its ability to retain water in the stool, which results in softer stools and promotes regular bowel movements.

subject: Pathology
question: Pirenzepine is used for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Glaucoma : Incorrect as Pirenzepine is not used to treat glaucoma. While muscarinic antagonists can affect eye conditions, pirenzepine’s specific action on gastric secretions does not extend to intraocular pressure control.
• Option C. Hypertension: Incorrect because Pirenzepine does not play a role in the management of blood pressure. Its pharmacological effects are confined to the digestive system.
• Option D. Congestive cardiac failure: Incorrect as Pirenzepine is not indicated for heart conditions like congestive cardiac failure . Its primary mechanism of action does not influence cardiac function or fluid management.
• Educational Objective:
• Pirenzepine is utilized in the management of gastric and duodenal ulcers due to its ability to selectively block M1 muscarinic receptors in the stomach, reducing gastric acid secretion which is beneficial for treating peptic ulcers.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is the drug of choice for treatment of chemotherapy-induced vomiting?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Prazosin: Incorrect because Prazosin is an alpha-1 blocker used primarily for managing hypertension and benign prostatic hyperplasia, not for vomiting.
• Option C. Clonidine: Incorrect as Clonidine is an alpha-2 agonist, used mainly for hypertension, ADHD, and anxiety disorders, and does not have a primary role in controlling chemotherapy-induced vomiting.
• Option D. Dimenhydrinate: Incorrect because Dimenhydrinate, an antihistamine, is primarily used for motion sickness and vertigo. While it can treat nausea and vomiting, it is not the drug of choice for chemotherapy-induced vomiting.
• Educational Objective:
• Granisetron is the drug of choice for managing chemotherapy-induced vomiting due to its effective inhibition of 5-HT3 receptors, critical in blocking the emetic pathway triggered by chemotherapy

subject: Pathology
question: What is the mechanism of action of teduglutide in short bowel syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. 5HT-1B/1D Agonist: Incorrect because 5HT-1B/1D agonists, such as triptans, are primarily used for migraine relief through vasoconstriction and inhibition of CGRP release, not for treating intestinal issues.
• Option C. GLP-1 analogs: Incorrect as GLP-1 analogs like exenatide and liraglutide are used in diabetes management due to their effects on insulin secretion and appetite suppression, rather than intestinal growth or nutrient absorption.
• Option D. C-peptide analogs: Incorrect because C-peptide analogs relate to insulin processing and are not used for treating conditions like short bowel syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Teduglutide acts as a GLP-2 analog, which is critical for promoting intestinal growth and enhancing the absorptive functions of the gut, making it effective in managing short bowel syndrome.

subject: Pathology
question: A patient present with chronic diarrhea and is prescribed an anti-diarrheal medication. Which of the following drugs acts by inhibiting 5-HT and is used specifically for irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea (IBS-D)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Clozapine: Incorrect because Clozapine is an atypical antipsychotic primarily used for the treatment of schizophrenia and is not used for treating diarrhea or IBS.
• Option C. Tegaserod: Incorrect as Tegaserod is a 5-HT4 receptor partial agonist, primarily used for the treatment of constipation-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-C) , not diarrhea.
• Option D. Mosapride: Incorrect because Mosapride is a 5-HT4 agonist used to enhance gastrointestinal motility , commonly prescribed for conditions like GERD and functional dyspepsia, but not specifically for IBS-D.
• Educational Objective:
• It is approved for the treatment of diarrhea predominant lBS in women. It is more potent antagonist of the 5-HT3 receptor than ondansetron. It shows significant improvements in abdominal pain as well as stool frequency, consistency and urgency in lBS patients. It also diminishes visceral sensitivity and slow colonic transit. Its major adverse effect is high incidence of development of ischemic colitis.

subject: Pathology
question: Identify the incorrect statement regarding proton pump inhibitors:
options: Acts from the luminal side of gastric parietal cells
Should be taken on empty stomach

explanation.short: Ans. B) Acts from the luminal side of gastric parietal cells
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Available as enteric coated tablets: This statement is correct . All PPIs are administered orally in enteric coated form to protect them from molecular transformation in the acidic gastric juice.
• Option C. Inactive at neutral pH: This statement is correct. Omeprazole is inactive at neutral pH , but at pH less than 5, it rearranges to two charged cationic forms (a sulphenic acid and a sulphenamide configurations ) that react covalently with SH groups of the H+K+ATPase enzyme and inactivate it irreversibly.
• Option D. Should be taken on an empty stomach: This statement is correct. Bioavailability of a ll PPIs is reduced by food; they should be taken on an empty stomach, followed 1 hour later by a meal to activate the H+K+ ATPase and make it more susceptible to the PPI.
• Educational Objective
• Proton pump inhibitors should be administered on an empty stomach to ensure maximum absorption and effectiveness . This should be followed by a meal an hour later to enhance the activation of gastric acid pumps, making them more susceptible to inhibition by the drug.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following drugs are not included in triple drug regimen for H. pylori associated peptic ulcer disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. PPI: Incorrect because proton pump inhibitors (such as Lansoprazole) are a crucial component of the triple therapy regimen, aiding in reducing gastric acid secretion to create a less acidic environment which enhances the effectiveness of antibiotics .
• Option B. Amoxicillin: Incorrect as Amoxicillin is one of the antibiotics recommended in the triple therapy , working by inhibiting the cell wall synthesis of H. pylori bacteria.
• Option D. Clarithromycin: Incorrect because Clarithromycin is included in the regimen and works by inhibiting protein synthesis of H. pylori , effectively helping in its eradication.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following best describes the rationale of prucalopride in management of constipation dominant IBS?
options: 5 HT3 antagonism resulting in decreased stimulation of extrinsic primary afferent neurons
5 HT1P antagonism resulting in decreased stimulation of intrinsic primary afferent neurons
5 HT4 agonism resulting in increased CGRP release and enhanced motility
Stimulation of type 2 chloride channel in small intestine increasing the volume of luminal contents.

explanation.short: Ans. C) 5 HT4 agonism resulting in increased CGRP release and enhanced motility
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 5 HT3 antagonism resulting in decreased stimulation of extrinsic primary afferent neurons: Incorrect as 5-HT3 antagonists are primarily used in the management of diarrhea-predominant IBS (IBS-D), where they help by reducing gut motility and fluid secretion.
• Option B. 5 HT1P antagonism resulting in decreased stimulation of intrinsic primary afferent neurons: Incorrect because there is no recognized 5-HT1P receptor in current serotonin receptor classification relevant to IBS management.
• Option D. Stimulation of type 2 chloride channel in small intestine increasing the volume of luminal contents: Incorrect as this describes the action of lubiprostone , not prucalopride. Lubiprostone works by activating type 2 chloride channels , leading to increased intestinal fluid secretion and stool passage.
• Educational Objective:
• Prucalopride enhances gastrointestinal motility through 5-HT4 agonism, which increases neurotransmitter release, notably CGRP, promoting enhanced peristalsis and improving symptoms of constipation-dominant IBS.

subject: Pathology
question: A 27-year-old woman who is 34 weeks pregnant is on bed rest and visits her obstetrician. During the visit, she informs her physician that she has been experiencing mild constipation. Which of the following medications will most likely be recommended to her?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Castor oil: Incorrect because castor oil is generally not recommended during pregnancy . It can trigger uterine contractions , which may pose risks to the fetus.
• Option C. Mineral oil: Incorrect as mineral oil is also not recommended during pregnancy . It can decrease the absorption of fat- s oluble vitamins , which are crucial for fetal development.
• Option D. Loperamide: Incorrect because loperamide is an anti-diarrheal medication, not a treatment for constipation. It works by slowing down gut movement, which would be counterproductive in the treatment of constipation.
• Educational Objective:
• Docusate is commonly recommended for treating constipation in pregnant women due to its efficacy and safety profile . It helps soften stools without the risks associated with other laxatives like castor oil and mineral oil , which can adversely affect pregnancy.

subject: Pathology
question: An elderly woman with a recent history of myocardial infarction is seeking a medication to help treat her occasional heartburn. She is currently taking several medications, including aspirin, clopidogrel, simvastatin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. Which of the following choices should be avoided in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Calcium citrate: This option is considered safe for treating heartburn and does not interact adversely with clopidogrel or the other medications the patient is taking.
• Option B. Famotidine: Famotidine is an H2-receptor antagonist and does not interfere with the CYP2C19 enzyme, making it a safe option for this patient.
• Option D. Ranitidine: Although ranitidine is also an H2-receptor antagonist, it has been found to be safe in terms of not significantly affecting the metabolism of clopidogrel like omeprazole does. However, due to other concerns such as potential NDMA contamination, ranitidine has been recalled in many markets.
• Educational Objective:
• While treating heartburn in patients on multiple medications, especially those on clopidogrel post-myocardial infarction, it is important to avoid proton pump inhibitors like omeprazole due to their potential to inhibit CYP2C19 and reduce the effectiveness of clopidogrel , thereby increasing the risk of cardiovascular events.

subject: Pathology
question: In emergency department, an intern gave an anti-emetic drug to a patient and the patient then developed abnormal posture and torticollis. Most likely drug is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ondansetron: Incorrect because Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist that is generally used to prevent nausea and vomiting associated with cancer chemotherapy and surgery and does not typically cause extrapyramidal side effects.
• Option B. Dexamethasone : Incorrect as Dexamethasone is a corticosteroid used as an antiemetic in chemotherapy s ettings. It works by reducing inflammation and does not cause extrapyramidal symptoms.
• Option D. Dronabinol: Incorrect because Dronabinol is a cannabinoid receptor agonist used to treat nausea and vomiting caused by chemotherapy and to stimulate appetite. Its side effects may include dysphoria, dizziness, and ataxia but not dystonia or torticollis.
• Educational Objective:
• Metoclopramide , while effective as an antiemetic and for promoting gastric motility , can cause extrapyramidal symptoms such as dystonia due to its antagonistic effects on dopamine D2 receptors . These effects are important to monitor, especially in emergency settings.

subject: Pathology
question: Which one of the following is the CFTR channel inhibitor?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A.  Linaclotide: Linaclotide is a guanylate cyclase-C agonist used in the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome with constipation and chronic idiopathic constipation . It works by increasing the secretion of chloride and bicarbonate ions in the intestine, leading to increased fluid secretion and accelerated gastrointestinal transit time.
• Option C. Alvimopan: Alvimopan is a peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonist used to treat postoperative ileus. It works by blocking the action of opioids on the mu-opioid receptor in the gastrointestinal tract, which can reduce gastrointestinal motility and cause constipation.
• Option D. Prucalopride : Prucalopride is a selective serotonin type 4 (5-HT4) receptor agonist used in the treatment of chronic idiopathic constipation . It works by stimulating colonic motility and accelerating gastrointestinal transit time, which can lead to increased bowel movements.
• Educational Objective:
• Crofelemer is unique as a CFTR channel inhibitor, making it effective in treating diarrhea by reducing chloride and water secretion into the intestines , which contrasts with other options that primarily affect motility or opioid receptor antagonism.

subject: Pathology
question: Selective M1 antagonist used in Gastric ulcer is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Omeprazole: Incorrect because Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) that works by irreversibly blocking the hydrogen/potassium ATPase enzyme in the stomach , leading to a significant reduction in gastric acid secretion. It does not act as a muscarinic antagonist.
• Option B. Ranitidine: Incorrect as Ranitidine is an H2 blocker that competitively inhibits histamine at H2 receptors on parietal cells, thereby decreasing gastric acid secretion . It is not a muscarinic antagonist.
• Option D. Misoprostol: Incorrect because Misoprostol is a mucosal protectant used primarily to prevent gastric ulcers caused by nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) by mimicking the action of prostaglandins . It does not involve muscarinic receptor antagonism.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old woman has been complaining of chronic constipation for several months. She has been taking a laxative regularly for relief.
