• The activated Partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) test evaluates the factors of the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways and is used to monitor heparin therapy, diagnose coagulation factor deficiencies , and assess the potential presence of inhibitors .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following are properties of neutrophils? Short living inflammatory response Involved in Neutrophil Extracellular trap (NET) formation Produces large quantities of nitric oxide Major site for production of cytokines

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Neutrophils contribute to early defense mechanisms in infection through rapid, potent inflammatory responses and the formation of NETs .
• Differences between the functions of neutrophils and macrophages
explanation.ref: Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of disease 10th ed- Pg: 81

subject: Pathology
question: Which one of the following is a false statement about Neutrophil Extracellular Trap (NETs)?
options: They are extracellular fibrillar networks preventing spread of microbes by trapping them
They provide high concentration of antimicrobial substances at site of infection
Neutrophils remain intact after producing the network

explanation.short: Ans. C) Neutrophils remain intact after producing the network
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. This statement is true . NETs are composed of decondensed chromatin and granular proteins , and they serve to trap bacteria, fungi, and other pathogens , thereby limiting their spread and facilitating their destruction .
• Option B. This statement is also true. NETs concentrate antimicrobial proteins from neutrophil granules, including enzymes like neutrophil elastase and myeloperoxidase , which help to neutralize pathogens.
• Option D. NETs are composed of DNA fibers that are expelled from the neutrophil and are associated with histones and other proteins , which give NETs their characteristic appearance and functionality.
• Educational Objective:
• NETosis , the process of forming Neutrophil Extracellular Traps , leads to the release of nuclear contents and cell death .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following are opsonins? Mannose binding lectin C3b C5a IL-6 IgG

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Opsonization is the process of coating a foreign particle and preparing it for phagocytosis . Important examples include:
• Mannose binding lectin C3b, C4b, C5b IgM and Fc receptor of IgG C-reactive protein (CRP) Fibrinogen
• Since C5a is a chemotactic meolecule and IL-6 is an acute phase reactant, so options A, B and D are incorrect.
• Educational Objective:
• Mannose-binding lectin, C3b, and IgG are opsonins that aid in the phagocytosis of pathogens.
explanation.ref: Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of disease 10th ed- Pg: 80

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not an antioxidant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Catalase is an enzyme found in nearly all living organisms exposed to oxygen . It catalyzes the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to water and oxygen, thereby protecting the cell from oxidative damage caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS). Thus, it acts as an antioxidant.
• Option B. Superoxide Dismutase (SOD) is an enzyme that protects the cell against harmful effects of superoxide radicals by catalyzing their conversion into less harmful molecules, oxygen and hydrogen peroxide . It is indeed an antioxidant enzyme .
• Option D. Transferrin is a glycoprotein that binds iron ions and transports them to different parts of the body. Iron-free transferrin, also known as apotransferrin , can bind free iron and, by doing so, limits iron-induced oxidative stress since free iron can catalyze the formation of harmful free radicals. Therefore, it has antioxidant properties .
• Educational Objective:
• Unlike the other options listed, phagocyte oxidase is not an antioxidant . Instead, it generates reactive oxygen species as part of the immune response to destroy pathogens.
explanation.ref: Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of disease 10th ed- Pg: 82

subject: Pathology
question: Major basic protein is seen in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Neutrophils , known for their role in the rapid response to bacterial infection, contain various granules filled with different enzymes and antimicrobial proteins . However, major basic protein is not one of them.
• Option C. Basophils , similar to mast cells , are involved in allergic reactions and contain histamine and heparin in their granules . They do not contain major basic protein.
• Option D. Macrophages are versatile cells involved in both phagocytosis and antigen presentation . They secrete a wide array of substances but do not contain major basic protein as a predominant component.
• Educational Objective:
• Major basic protein is a key component of eosinophils and has a role in the immune response , particularly in the destruction of helminthic parasites and in allergic reactions .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not present in the primary granules of neutrophils:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options A, B and C are components of primary neutrophilic granules.
• Educational Objective:
• Lactoferrin is a component of secondary granules present in neutrophils .
explanation.ref: Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of disease 10th ed- Pg: 82

subject: Pathology
question:
A 1 year old boy had complaints of recurrent fever. He had been treated for severe pneumonia 3 weeks back. Clinical examination revealed mild pallor, oral thrush, micrognathia, low-set right ear. Skin examination was remarkable with patchy hypopigmentation around face, trunk, back, abdomen, perianal region, hands and feet along with hypopigmented silver gray hair. Peripheral smear showed abnormal inclusions as shown in the given image. Identify the defect associated with this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/05/picture2_9FQIxoB.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Seen in Chronic granulomatous disease.
• Option C. MPO deficiency - This condition has minimal susceptibility to infection .
• Option D. Associated with X-linked SCID (Severe combined immunodeficiency disease)
• Educational Objective:
• Chediak-Higashi syndrome is diagnosed by the presence of giant granules in leukocytes and is caused by a defect in the fusion of phagosomes with lysosomes , leading to impaired microbial killing .
explanation.ref: Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of disease 10th ed- Pg: 82,83

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following statements about reactive oxygen species is true?
options: MPO catalyzes the conversion of hydrogen peroxide to hypochlorite
Catalases in peroxisomes help in clearing hydroxyl radicals
Superoxide anions are generated during Fenton reaction
Manganese superoxide dismutase is located in the cytoplasm

explanation.short: Ans. A) MPO catalyzes the conversion of hydrogen peroxide to hypochlorite
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options B. Catalase enzyme decomposes hydroge peroxide to ater and oxygen
• Option C. Hydroxyl radicals are produced during fenton reaction from superoxide anions .
• Option D. Manganese superoxide dismutase is located in the mitochondria
• Educational Objective:
• MPO catalyzes the conversion of hydrogen peroxide to hypochlorite .
explanation.ref: Pg no.52-53, 82-83, Robbins and Cotran Pathologic basis of diseases 10th edition

subject: Pathology
question: All are functions of integrins except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Integrins on platelets, specifically αIIbβ3 , are central to platelet aggregation . When activated, they bind to fibrinogen, which allows platelets to cross-link with each other, a fundamental step in clot formation .
• Option B. Diapedesis is the process of leukocytes moving through the endothelial junctions of blood vessels to enter the tissue at an inflammation site . Integrins on leukocytes mediate their firm adhesion to the endothelial cells before transmigration.
• Option C. Integrins facilitate cell-cell adhesive interactions by binding to cell-adhesion molecules on other cells, which is critical for cellular communication and migration .
• Educational Objective
• Integrins play a role in specific aspects of wound healing related to cell adhesion and migration but do not encompass the entire process of wound healing, which involves complex interactions and the contribution of various molecules and cell types.
• Allow cells to attach t o laminin and fibronectin thus linking the intracellular cytoskeleton with the outside world . Facilitate cell-cell adhesive interactions . Mediate firm adhesion and migration of leukocytes across endothelium and epithelium at sites of inflammation . Play a critical role in platelet aggregation

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a painful and swollen left leg that has been gradually worsening over the past week. He reports a history of recent surgery on his left leg. Which of the following mechanisms is responsible for the edema formation in the affected leg?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Vasodilation is the widening of blood vessels . While it is a component of inflammation and can contribute to the initial stages of swelling by increasing blood flow to the affected area , it does not directly cause the protein-rich fluid leakage that results in sustained edema.
• Option C. Chemotaxis is the movement of immune cells towards a chemical signal , often seen in the context of inflammation where cells migrate towards higher concentrations of certain chemokines released at the site of injury or infection . It is a cellular migratory mechanism and does not directly lead to edema.
• Option D. Opsonization is a process by which pathogens are marked for destruction by the immune system . This process enhances the ability of phagocytes to recognize, engulf, and destroy pathogens and does not relate to the development of edema.
• Educational Objective:
• The correct answer is A) Increased Vascular Permeability. Post-surgical edema is typically due to increased vascular permeability , which is part of the inflammatory response allowing fluid and proteins to leak into the interstitial tissue, leading to swelling.

subject: Pathology
question: An elderly coal miner with a history of smoking and emphysema develops severe air-flow obstruction and expires. His autopsy reveals a “black lung,” with coal-dust nodules scattered throughout the parenchyma and a central area of dense fibrosis. The coal dust entrapped within this miner’s lung was sequestered primarily by which of the following cells?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Endothelial cells line the blood vessels and are involved in the exchange of substances between the bloodstream and surrounding tissues.
• Option B. Fibroblasts are the main cell type involved in tissue repair and fibrosis . In response to lung injury, they produce the extracellular matrix and collagen leading to fibrosis, but they do not phagocytose foreign materials such as coal dust .
• Option C. Lymphocytes , including T and B cells , are primarily involved in the immune response against pathogens and are not known for phagocytosing materials like coal dust .
• Educational Objective
• In conditions such as coal workers' pneumoconiosis , also known as "black lung ," macrophages are the primary cells responsible for sequestering coal dust . They phagocytose the dust particles and become the centre of coal-dust nodules , which may lead to lung fibrosis.

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old female presents with wheezing, shortness of breath, and cough. She has a history of asthma and is afebrile. On examination, her lungs show diffuse wheezing and decreased air entry. Which of the following chemical mediators is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of her inflammation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Prostaglandins are eicosanoids derived from arachidonic acid that can cause vasodilation and increase the permeability of blood vessels . They can play a role in inflammation and are associated with pain and fever , but they are not the most significant mediators in bronchoconstriction associated with asthma.
• Option C. Histamine is released by mast cells and plays a significant role in immediate hypersensitivity reactions . It can cause bronchoconstriction, increase vascular permeability, and cause smooth muscle contraction, but it is not the primary mediator in ongoing asthmatic responses.
• Option D. IL-6 is a cytokine involved in the acute-phase reaction and plays a role in chronic inflammation and autoimmunity . While it is involved in systemic inflammation, it is not a major direct mediator of bronchoconstriction in asthma.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of asthma, leukotrienes are critical mediators that contribute to bronchial constriction, mucus secretion, and airway inflammation, leading to symptoms like wheezing and shortness of breath.

subject: Pathology
question: A 50-year-old male presents with a history of chronic gastritis and recurrent episodes of epigastric pain. Which of the following chemical mediators is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of his symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Prostaglandins are eicosanoids derived from arachidonic acid that can cause vasodilation and increase the permeability of blood vessels . They can play a role in maintaining the integrity of the gastric mucosa by promoting mucus and bicarbonate secretion, as well as by increasing mucosal blood flow. They also inhibit gastric acid secretion . Chronic gastritis , particularly if caused by prolonged use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or Helicobacter pylori infection, can lead to a decreas e in prostaglandin levels . This decrease in prostaglandins can disrupt the mucosal barrier, leading to increased susceptibility to gastri c damage and ulcer formation . Therefore, in this patient with chronic gastritis and recurrent epigastric pain , the involvement of prostaglandins in the pathogenesis of his symptoms is most likely.
• Educational Objective:
• Understanding the role of prostaglandins in maintaining gastric mucosal integrity and how their depletion can contribute to the development of gastritis and associated symptoms, such as epigastric pain .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following complement component can be activated is both common as well as alternative pathways?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. C1 is the first component of the classical pathway of complement activation . It is not involved in the alternative pathway , which is initiated by spontaneous hydrolysis or microbial surface constituents .
• Option B. C2 is part of the classical and lectin pathways . It forms a complex with C4 to produce the C3 convertase C4b2a . C2 is not involved in the alternative pathway.
• Option D. C4 is involved in the classical and lectin pathways where, along with C2 , it forms the C3 convertase . It is not part of the alternative pathway, which bypasses C4 .
• Educational Objective:
• C3 is a key component in the complement system that is activated by all three pathways: classical, lectin, and alternative. Its activation is pivotal in the complement-mediated immune response , including opsonization and formation of the membrane attack complex.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not an inflammatory mediator?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. TNF is a cytokine that plays a crucial role in inflammation. It is involved in systemic inflammation and is one of the cytokines that make up the acute phase reaction. TNF can cause fever, apoptotic cell death, sepsis , and it can stimulate the acute phase reaction .
• Option C. Interferons are a group of signaling proteins made and released by host cells in response to the presence of pathogens such as viruses, bacteria, parasites, or tumor cells. IFNs allow communication between cells to trigger the protective defenses of the immune system that help clear pathogens. They are considered inflammatory mediators due to their role in immune activation.
• Option D. Interleukins are a group of cytokines that were first seen to be expressed by leukocytes. They are a key component of the immune system and play important roles in initiating and regulating inflammation, cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis .
• Educational Objective:
• While MPO has a role in pathogen destruction by neutrophils , it is not traditionally considered a mediator of inflammation in the same way that cytokines and interferons are, which actively regulate the inflammatory response.

subject: Pathology
question: Nephrocalcinosis in a systemic granulomatous disease is due to:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Overproduction of 1,25 dihydroxy vitamin D
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Dystrophic calcification occurs in areas of necrosis or damage, irrespective of serum calcium levels . This type of calcification is not directly related to systemic granulomatous disease or vitamin D metabolism.
• Option C. Mutations in calcium-sensing receptors (CaSR) can lead to alterations in calcium homeostasis . However, CaSR mutations typically cause disorders such as familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, not nephrocalcinosis in the context of granulomatous disease.
• Option D. While increased reabsorption of calcium in the kidneys can contribute to hypercalcemia, it is not typically due to systemic granulomatous disease . It may occur as a secondary effect of hypercalcemia from other causes, such as primary hyperparathyroidism.
• Educational Objective:
• In systemic granulomatous diseases, the overproduction of the active form o f vitamin D by macrophages leads to increased calcium absorption and can result in nephrocalcinosis due to calcium deposition in the kidneys .

subject: Pathology
question: Bradykinin is associated with all of the following actions in acute inflammation excep:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bradykinin is well known for causing pain . It binds to receptors on pain fibers , leading to their activation . This is a common and well-recognized effect of bradykinin.
• Option B. One of the primary actions of bradykinin is causing vasodilation . It acts on vascular smooth muscles and leads to the relaxation of blood vessels , contributing to the increased blood flow observed in inflammation.
• Option D. Bradykinin increases vascular permeability , allowing fluid, proteins, and cells to leave the blood vessels and move into the tissues , contributing to the swelling and edema associated with inflammation.
• Educational Objective:
• Bradykinin is involved in increasing vascular permeability , causing arteriolar dilation, bronchial smooth muscle contraction, and pain , but not vasoconstriction.

subject: Pathology
question: A small boy injured his thumb with a rusty nail. Few hours later, the thumb appears red and swollen. Initial swelling of the boy’s thumb is primarily due to which of the following mechanisms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Decreased hydrostatic pressure inside blood vessels would not lead to edema ; instead, it would potentially decrease the tendency for fluid to leave the capillaries.
• Option B. Decreased permeability of venules would also not be expected to cause swelling , as it would reduce the leakage of fluid from the vessels into the surrounding tissue.
• Option D. Oncotic pressure is the osmotic pressure exerted by proteins (e.g., albumin) in the blood plasma . An increase in oncotic pressure would tend to pull fluid into the vessels, not out into the tissues, and therefore would not cause swelling .
• Educational Objective:
• Following tissue injury and subsequent inflammation , increased capillary permeability allows fluid and proteins to leak out into the tissue , resulting in the clinical presentation of swelling and redness.

subject: Pathology
question: A 20-year-old male presented with weight loss, low grade fever and cough with expectoration. Sputum testing for acid fast bacilli was positive, which of the following cytokines are involved in the pathogenesis of this disease:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D. IL4 & IL 13 activated macrophages are alternatively activated macrophages and they are involved in tissue repair and termination of inflammation.
• Option A. and B. They are anti-inflammatory cytokines
• Educational Objective:
• In tuberculosis, IFN-γ plays a crucial role in the activation of macrophages and the formation of granulomas, which are central to the body's defense against the bacilli .

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old male presents with redness, swelling, and pain in his right knee. He reports a history of gout and takes allopurinol. Which of the following chemical mediators is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of his acute inflammation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Leukotriene B4 (LTB4) is an eicosanoid that acts as a potent chemoattractant and activator of neutrophils . It is involved in various inflammatory responses, including gout , but is not the primary cytokine implicated in the acute phase of gouty arthritis.
• Option B. TNF-α is a pro-inflammatory cytokine produced primarily by macrophages. It has a wide range of actions, including the induction o f fever, apoptotic cell death, and inflammation. While TNF-α is involved in gouty inflammation, it is not the most directly implicated in the crystalline-induced inflammation seen in gout.
• Option D. Histamine is involved in immediate allergic responses and contributes to th e vasodilation and increased vascular permeability seen in the early stages of inflammation. In the context of gout, histamine may contribute to the initial response but is not a key mediator in the ongoing inflammatory process.
• Educational Objective
• In the context of gout, IL-1 is critical for the inflammatory response to monosodium urate crystals in joints. IL-1 inhibitors are also used in the treatment of gouty arthritis, highlighting the central role of IL-1 in the disease process.

subject: Pathology
question: A 35-year-old female presents with fever, cough, and shortness of breath. Her chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following chemical mediators is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of her acute inflammation?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Leukotriene B4 (LTB4) is involved in the inflammatory process , mainly by attracting neutrophils to sites of inflammation . While it is a potent chemoattractant and can contribute to inflammation in the lungs , it is not the primary mediator in ARDS.
• Option C. IL-1 is another important pro-inflammatory cytokine and plays a role similar to TNF-α in promoting inflammation . However, it is not as specifically implicated in ARDS as TNF-α.
• Option D. Histamine is a mediator of allergic reactions and contributes to bronchoconstriction and increased vascular permeability . While histamine may be involved in the early stages of some types of lung injury , particularly those with an allergic or anaphylactic component, it is not the primary mediator in ARDS.
• Educational Objective:
• TNF-α is a critical mediator in the pathogenesis of acute lung injury and ARDS , promoting the recruitment of inflammatory cells to the lungs and contributing to the tissue damage and impaired gas exchange characteristic of these conditions.

subject: Pathology
question: A 50-year-old female presents with fever, cough, and shortness of breath. Her chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following factors in the coagulation cascade is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of her acute inflammation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Factor II , or prothrombin , is a plasma protein that is converted into thrombin in the coagulation cascade. Thrombin is a key enzyme that converts fibrinogen to fibrin, leading to clot formation. While it is central to the coagulation process, it is not known for initiating the inflammatory response in acute lung injury.
• Option B. Factor V is a coagulation factor that works with factor X to convert prothrombin to thrombin . It is important in the progression of the coagulation cascade, but again, it is not specifically involved in the initiation of the inflammatory process in lung injury.
• Option C. Factor VII is involved in initiating the extrinsic pathway of the coagulation cascade upon tissue injury . While it is crucial for the body's response to vascular injury , it is not directly involved in the inflammation associated with acute lung injury.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of acute lung injury , the activation of Factor XII can lead to the formation of fibrin clots in the pulmonary vasculature and contribute to the inflammatory response through the production of bradykinin.

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old male presents with fever, fatigue, and joint pain. He reports a history of intravenous drug use and is found to have a positive blood culture for Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following systems is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of his acute inflammation?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The complement system is a part of the innate immune system that enhances the ability of antibodies and phagocytic cells to clear microbes and damaged cells, promotes inflammation, and attacks the pathogen's cell membrane . It is often activated in bacterial infections and plays a role in the opsonization of pathogens , recruitment of inflammatory cells, and direct killing of bacteria via the membrane attack complex.
• Option B. The kinin system leads to the production of bradykinin , a potent vasodilator that increases vascular permeability and contributes to the pain associated with inflammation . The kinin system works in conjunction with other inflammatory pathways to contribute to the inflammatory response .
• Option C. The clotting system or coagulation cascade can be triggered by inflammatory responses and tissue damage . It contributes to the containment of infection by trapping pathogens within fibrin clots and can lead to the activation of certain aspects of the immune response.
• Educational Objective:
• In the setting of a systemic bacterial infection , the complement system, kinin system, and clotting system are all implicated in the pathogenesis of acute inflammation. These systems work in concert to control the spread of infection, recruit immune cells, and create an environment conducive to the destruction of the pathogen

subject: Pathology
question: A 35-year-old female presents with a painful and tender nodule on her shin. She reports a history of tuberculosis and was treated successfully. A biopsy of the lesion shows a granulomatous inflammation with giant cells. What type of giant cells is most likely present in her lesion?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Foreign body giant cells arise when foreign materials, too large to be phagocytosed by a single macrophage, are present in the tissue. Their nuclei are haphazardly arranged within the cell , and these cells are often associated with chronic inflammation due to persistent foreign bodies.
• Option C. Touton giant cells are typically found in fat necrosis and some tumors . They have a ring of nuclei surrounded by foamy cytoplasm and are not typically associated with granulomatous inflammation from tuberculosis.
• Option D . Warthin-Finkeldey giant cells are multinucleated giant cells found in the hyperplastic lymphoid tissue , commonly associated with measles and other viral infections , not tuberculosis.
• Educational Objective:
• In a patient with a history of tuberculosis , Langhans giant cells are indicative of a granulomatous response to mycobacterial antigens. These cells are formed from the fusion of macrophages and are characteristic of the granulomas seen in tuberculosis.

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old male presents with erythema nodosum and polyarthralgia. Chest x-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Biopsy of the lymph nodes shows non-caseating granulomas. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tuberculosis, caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis , typically presents with caseating granulomas , not non-caseating. It can cause similar symptoms and chest radiograph findings, but the granulomas in tuberculosis usually exhibit central necrosis .
• Option C. Histoplasmosis is a fungal infection that can cause granulomatous inflammation . It often presents with symptoms similar to tuberculosis and can affect the lungs and lymph nodes . However, it is more commonly associated with caseating granulomas and typically occurs in endemic areas .
• Option D. Hodgkin's lymphoma is a type of lymphoma that can present with lymphadenopathy ; however, it is diagnosed by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells in the lymph nodes , not non-caseating granulomas.
• Educational Objective:
• This diagnosis of sarcoidosis is supported by the clinical presentation of erythema nodosum, polyarthralgia, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, and biopsy findings of non-caseating granulomas.

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old female presents with a painless mass on her thigh. She reports a history of trauma to the area several years ago. Imaging studies show a well-defined mass with calcifications. A biopsy of the lesion shows a granulomatous inflammation with giant cells. Which of the following mechanisms is most likely responsible for the development of granulomatous inflammation in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chronic smoking can cause a variety of pulmonary problems and has been associated with an increased risk of developing granulomas in the lungs . However, it is less likely to cause a localized granulomatous reaction in the soft tissue of the thigh as a response to an old injury.
• Option B. Immune dysregulation can lead to granulomatous diseases like sarcoidosis, where the immune system creates granulomas in various organs without a known foreign body or pathogen. This systemic process usually doesn't result in an isolated thigh mass related to a previous trauma.
• Educational Objective:
• In the setting of a prior trauma with subsequent development of a mass , the biopsy showing granulomatous inflammation with giant cells is most consistent with a foreign body reaction.

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old female presents with a painful and tender nodule on her lower leg. She reports a history of travel to an endemic area for leprosy and was treated successfully. A biopsy of the lesion shows a granulomatous inflammation with giant cells. What is the pathophysiology underlying this patient's condition?
options: Genetic defect in the NADPH oxidase enzyme

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Enzyme A genetic defect in NADPH oxidase results in chronic granulomatous disease , a condition where phagocytes can ingest but cannot kill certain bacteria and fungi due to a lack of reactive oxygen species production. This is different from the immune response to leprosy.
• Option C. Environmental toxins can lead to various forms of inflammation and tissue damage , but they are not typically associated with granulomatous inflammation due to an infectious disease like leprosy.
• Option D. Chronic viral infections can lead to persistent immune responses and sometimes granulomatous inflammation ; however, leprosy is a bacterial infection , not viral.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of leprosy, granulomatous inflammation is a result of the body's immune response to the intracellular pathogen, Mycobacterium leprae . The formation of granulomas , often containing giant cells , is a way the immune system attempts to contain the pathogen.

subject: Pathology
question: A 25-year-old male presents with recurrent skin abscesses and infections. His medical history is significant for chronic granulomatous disease (CGD). What is the underlying pathophysiology of CGD?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Defects in phagocytic cell killing
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• CGD is an inherited disorder of the immune system that affects the function of phagocytic cells, such as neutrophils and macrophages . The underlying defect in CGD is a deficiency in one of the components of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) oxidase complex , which is responsible for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) that are necessary for the killing of ingested microorganisms. As a result, individuals with CGD are more susceptible to bacterial and fungal infections, as well as chronic granulomatous inflammation.

subject: Pathology
question: A 40-year-old female presents with cough and shortness of breath. She reports a history of exposure to birds and bird droppings. A chest x-ray shows bilateral nodular infiltrates, and a biopsy of the lung shows granulomatous inflammation. What is the underlying pathophysiology of the granulomatous inflammation in this patient?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Defects in phagocytic cell activation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. While migration to the site of infection is essential for the immune response , CGD specifically involves a defect in the killing of pathogens after phagocytosis, rather than the migration of phagocytic cells.
• Option C. CGD is caused by a genetic defect in the NADPH oxidase complex , which is critical for the respiratory burst in phagocytes that produces reactive oxygen species necessary for the killing of pathogens . Patients with CGD cannot effectively destroy certain bacteria and fungi, leading to recurrent infections and granuloma formation.
• Option D. CGD is not related to the proliferation of phagocytic cells ; it involves the cells' functionality post-phagocytosis, particularly their ability to kill ingested pathogens.
• Educational Objective:
• The pathophysiology of CGD is due to a defect in the NADPH oxidase complex within phagocytes, leading to impaired killing of ingested bacteria and fungi . This results in an increased susceptibility to infections and the formation of granulomas.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following serum proteins activates the complement, coagulation, and fibrinolytic systems at the site of injury in the patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bradykinin is a peptide produced as a result of kallikrein's action on kininogen . While bradykinin contributes to vasodilation and increased vascular permeability , it is not involved in the activation of the complement or coagulation systems.
• Option C. Kallikrein is an enzyme that comes from prekallikrein, which is indeed activated by Hageman factor. It plays a role in the generation of bradykinin but does not directly activate the complement or coagulation systems.
• Option D. Plasmin is involved in fibrinolysis , breaking down clots after they have formed. Its precursor, plasminogen, is converted to plasmin by the action of activators, one of which is the Hageman factor-mediated pathway .
• Educational Objective
• Activation of Hageman factor at the site of injury initiates processes that balance coagulation and fibrinolysis , control local blood flow, and contribute to the innate immune response .

subject: Pathology
question: A 20-year-old man sustains a deep, open laceration over his left thigh in a motorcycle accident. The wound is cleaned and sutured. Which of the following cell types mediates contraction of the wound to facilitate healing?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Endothelial cells respond to growth factors and form capillaries, which are necessary for the delivery of nutrients and inflammatory cells .
• Option B. Fibroblasts tend to be solitary cells, surrounded by collagen fibers .
• Option C . Macrophages or smooth muscle cells mediate wound contraction
• Educational Objective:
• Myofibroblasts are responsible for wound contraction, which helps reduce the size of the wound and is essential for wound healing.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 40-year-old woman presents with a painless lesion on her ear lobe (shown in the image) after getting her ears pierced 3 months ago. Which of the following best explains the pathogenesis of this lesion?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/06/picture1.jpg]
options: Increased turnover of extracellular matrix proteoglycans
Increased growth of capillary endothelial cells

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. While extracellular matrix components , including proteoglycans, play a role in wound healing , their turnover is not the primary defect in keloid formation.
• Option B. Granulation tissue is the new connective tissue and microscopic blood vessels that form on the surfaces of a wound during the healing process . Exuberant granulation tissue can lead to hypertrophic scars , which are similar to keloids but do not grow beyond the boundaries of the original wound.
• Option C. This process is more related to the formation of granulation tissue during wound healing . Although it is an essential part of the healing process, it does not specifically lead to keloids.
• Educational Objective:
• In keloids , there is an imbalance in the synthesis and degradation of collagen , with an increase in the ratio of type III to type I collagen . This suggests a failure in the normal maturation process of collagen during wound healing , leading to the excessive scar formation typical of keloids .

subject: Pathology
question: A school boy scrapes the palms of his hands. The wounds are cleaned and covered with sterile gauze. Which of the following terms best characterizes the healing of these superficial abrasions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Healing by secondary intention occurs when a wound is left open and closes naturally by the process of granulation tissue formation, contraction, and epithelialization. It is typical for deeper wounds, extensive damage, or when the edges cannot be brought together .
• Option B. Granulation tissue is the new connective tissue and tiny blood vessels that form on the surface of a wound during the healing process . It typically occurs in deeper wounds that need to fill in with new tissue.
• Option C. Healing by primary intention happens when the wound edges are brought together , as in a surgical incision or a clean cut . The edges are approximated and heal with minimal scar formation.
• Educational Objective:
• Superficial abrasions heal by regeneration because they involve only the epidermis or the upper dermis, which can fully restore the normal tissue organization and function . The process is mediated by the migration of epithelial cells to cover the wound surface.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following nutritional factors is required for proper collagen assembly in the scar tissue of the patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Folic acid is crucial for DNA synthesis and repair, and cell division . It is particularly important in rapidly dividing tissues, like those in a healing wound , but it is not directly involved in collagen synthesis.
• Option B. Thiamine (Vitamin B1) is important in carbohydrate metabolism and energy production , but it does not play a direct role in collagen synthesis or wound healing.
• Option C. Vitamin A is involved in cell proliferation, immune function, and inflammatory response. It can help with wound healing and has been shown to reverse the inhibition of collagen synthesis that is caused by steroids . However, its role is not in the hydroxylation of proline residues in collagen.
• Educational Objective:
• Vitamin C is required for the hydroxylation of proline and lysine in collagen synthesis , making it essential for proper collagen assembly and wound healing . Deficiency in vitamin C leads to scurvy , characterized by poor wound healing and abnormal collagen with decreased tensile strength .

subject: Pathology
question: A 35-year-old female presents with joint pain, stiffness, and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports a history of psoriasis and has been diagnosed with psoriatic arthritis. Which cytokine is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of her disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. IL-6 is another pro-inflammatory cytokine involved in the immune response and inflammation . While it plays a role in the pathophysiology of various arthritic conditions, including rheumatoid arthritis, TNF-α is more specifically targeted in the treatment of psoriatic arthritis.
• Option C. IL-1β is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that contributes to inflammation and cellular damage in many autoimmune diseases. Like TNF-α and IL-6 , it is involved in the inflammatory cascade but is not as prominent a target in psoriatic arthritis as TNF-α.
• Option D. IL-10 is an anti-inflammatory cytokine that helps to regulate the immune response and dampen inflammation . It's typically not the target in psoriatic arthritis treatment strategies but rather part of the body's natural mechanism to control excessive inflammation .
• Educational Objective:
• TNF-α i s critically involved in the inflammatory processes of psoriatic arthritis and is a primary target for therapeutic intervention . Biologic agents that inhibit TNF-α, such as infliximab , have been effective in reducing the inflammation and joint damage associated with psoriatic arthritis .

subject: Pathology
question: Function of hepatic stellate cells is/are:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hepatic sinusoids are specialized blood vessels within the liver that facilitate exchange between the blood and hepatocytes . Sinusoid formation involves complex interactions between various cell types during liver development.
• Option C: Hepatic stellate cells do not directly regulate blood perfusion in the liver . Blood perfusion within the liver is primarily regulated by hepatic blood flow dynamics , which involve interactions between hepatic arteries, portal veins, and hepatic sinusoids.
• Option D: Phagocytic activity in the liver is primarily carried out by Kupffer cells , which are specialized macrophages located within the hepatic sinusoids . Kupffer cells play a crucial role in clearing pathogens, cellular debris, and foreign particles from the bloodstream, contributing to liver defense and immune function.
• Educational objective:
• Hepatic stellate cells have diverse functions , including their involvement in liver fibrosis , the primary function attributed to them is the storage and metabolism of vitamin A .

subject: Pathology
question: After passing his physical exam, a young army recruit gives urine and blood samples for further testing. Serum analysis yields elevated ALT, HBsAg, Anti-HBc, HBeAg, and bilirubin. All other values are normal. Which of the following is the hepatitis B status of this recruit?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: An asymptomatic carrier does not have ele­vated ALT and bilirubin .
• Option C: The absence of findings on physical examination rules out fulminant hepatitis B
• Option D: Recovery from acute self-limited HBV is asso­ciated with the presence of anti-HBs and the decrease in HBsAg and HBeAg .
• Educational objective:
• The presence of elevated ALT , HBsAg, anti-HBc , HBeAg, and bilirubin are indicative of active hepatitis B infection .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following condition is associated with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Dubin-Johnson syndrome: it is characterized by conjugated hyperbilirubinemia . It is a rare genetic disorder where there is impaired excretion of conjugated bilirubin from the liver.
• Option B: Rotor syndrome: it is also associated with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia . It is a benign condition characterized by elevated levels of both conjugated and unconjugated bilirubin.
• Option D: Gall stones: they can lead to obstructive jaundice , causing elevated levels of both conjugated and unconjugated bilirubin . However, the primary association is with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
• Educational Objective:
• Gilbert syndrome is a common inherited condition associated with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.

subject: Pathology
question: A patient with unconjugated bilirubinemia has increased excretion of urobilinogen in his urine. This can be seen in all of the following conditions, except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: G6PD deficiency can lead to hemolysis , resulting in increased unconjugated bilirubin production . Therefore, it is associated with increased urobilinogen excretion.
• Option B: Hemolytic anemia, where red blood cells are destroyed prematurely , increase the production of unconjugated bilirubin . This can lead to increased urobilinogen in the urine.
• Option C: Hereditary spherocytosis is a hemolytic disorder characterized by spherical-shaped red blood cells , leading to increased bilirubin production . It is associated with increased urobilinogen excretion.
• Educational objective:
• Biliary cirrhosis is associated with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia .

subject: Pathology
question: In post-hepatic jaundice, the concentration of conjugated bilirubin in the blood is higher than that of unconjugated bilirubin because:
options: There is an increased rate of destruction of red blood cells
The unconjugated bilirubin is trapped by the bile stone produced in the bile duct
The conjugation process of bilirubin in liver remains operative without any interference
The UDP glucuronyl transferase activity is increased manifold in obstructive jaundice

explanation.short: Ans. C) The conjugation process of bilirubin in liver re­mains operative without any interference
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Increased destruction of red blood cells (hemolysis) primarily leads to unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia , not conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
• Option B: Bile stones (gallstones) can cause obstruction and may lead to obstructive jaundice , but the bilirubin trapped is primarily conjugated, not unconjugated.
• Option D: In obstructive jaundice , there is no direct increase in UDP glucuronyl transferase activity . The elevated levels of conjugated bilirubin result from impaired excretion rather than increased conjugation.
• Educational objective:
• In post-hepatic jaundice , the concentration of conjugated bilirubin in the blood is higher than that of unconjugated bilirubin because the flow of bile is obstructed , leading to the accumulation of conjugated bilirubin in the blood.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following diseases is not a cause of indirect hyperbilirubinemia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Criggler Najjar syndrome is a hereditary disorder characterized by deficiency or absence of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase enzyme activity , leading to impaired conjugation of bilirubin . It is a cause of indirect hyperbilirubinemia.
• Option C: Gilbert syndrome is a common inherited disorder characterized by mild deficiency of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase enzyme activity , resulting in reduced conjugation of bilirubin . It is a cause of indirect hyperbilirubinemia.
• Option D: Hereditary spherocytosis is a hemolytic disorder characterized by spherical-shaped red blood cells . It can lead to increased production of unconjugated bilirubin due to premature destruction of red blood cells (hemolysis). Therefore, it is not a cause of indirect hyperbilirubinemia.
• Educational objective:
• Rotor’s syndrome is a cause of direct hyperbilirubinemia .

subject: Pathology
question: A 30-year-old woman Aishwarya goes to her gynecologist Dr. Raina Chawla for a pre-pregnancy examination. Routine prenatal laboratory testing demonstrates normal hematological profile with controlled sugar as well negative TORCH infections. She normal liver function tests with the following profile: HBsAg negative, anti- HBcAg (-), anti- HBeAg (-), HBV DNA polymerase (-) but anti- HBsAg is positive. Which of the following likely represents the status of the patient?
options: Infected with hepatitis B and highly transmissible

explanation.short: Ans. C) Immunized against hepatitis B
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hepatitis B carrier: This status would be indicated by the presence of hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) in the serum for more than six months . A carrier is also often positive for hepatitis B e antigen (HBeAg) or has detectable levels of HBV DNA, reflecting ongoing replication of the virus. However, this patient is negative for HBsAg, HBeAg, and HBV DNA polymerase, so she is not a carrier.
• Option C: Immunized against hepatitis B: The presence of antibodies to HBsAg (anti-HBs) without the presence of HBsAg or anti-HBc suggests immunity from vaccination . This is because the hepatitis B vaccine contains HBsAg, which stimulates the production of anti-HBs without causing an infection.
• Option D: Infected with hepatitis B and highly transmissible: If the patient were infected and highly infectious , we would expect to see positive HBeAg and HBV DNA polymerase since these are markers of high levels of viral replication and infectivity . However, this patient is negative for both.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of anti-HBsAg in the absence of HBsAg and other markers of infection indicates that the patient has been successfully immunized against hepatitis B. The hepatitis B vaccine induces the production of anti-HBs antibodies, which provide immunity to the virus.

subject: Pathology
question: An eminent hepatobiliary expert Dr. Sarin conducts a study in hepatitis B patients for which the patients are followed for almost a decade. Their detailed history regarding the mode of transmission of HBV is taken. A battery of tests including periodic serologic testing for HBs Ag, anti HBs and anti-HBc, and serum levels of bilirubin, SOT, SGPT, alkaline phosphatase, and prothrombin time is conducted. Dr. Sarin finds that a particular group of patients happen to be chronic carriers of HBV. This finding is most likely to be associated with which of the following modes of transmission of HBV?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Vertical transmission during childbirth
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Blood transfusion: Though hepatitis B can be transmitted through blood transfusions , the risk is significantly reduced due to rigorous screening protocols for blood and blood products . Chronic carriage from this mode is less likely in the current era due to these safeguards.
• Option B: Heterosexual transmission: Hepatitis B can be spread through sexual contact ; however, chronic carrier status is less commonly associated with this mode of transmission compared to others, particularly vertical transmission during childbirth.
• Option D: Needle stick injury: While hepatitis B can be transmitted via needle stick injuries , and the risk is higher compared to HIV and HCV, the development of chronic carrier status from this mode is less frequent than with vertical transmission.
• Educational Objective:
• Chronic carrier status of HBV is most closely associated with vertical transmission due to the lack of a mature immune response in newborns , which is necessary to clear the virus and prevent chronic infection .

subject: Pathology
question: A 34-year-old man Bholu presents to his physician with loss of appetite, nausea and vomiting, and fatigue. Laboratory examination confirms the diagnosis of hepatitis B, and the man becomes icteric 2 weeks later. This patient may also be particularly vulnerable to the development of which of the following disorders?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Berry aneurysm: A berry aneurysm is typically associated with conditions like polycystic kidney disease and hypertension , and it predominantly affects the cerebral vasculature . It is not commonly linked to hepatitis B infection.
• Option B: Coronary artery aneurysm : This type of aneurysm is often associated with Kawasaki disease or atherosclerosis , and there is no direct link to hepatitis B infection for the development of coronary artery aneurysms.
• Option D: Giant cell arteritis : Giant cell arteritis typically affects the large and medium arteries of older adults , particularly the temporal arteries, and is not associated with hepatitis B infection.
• Educational Objective:
• There is a well-established association between hepatitis B infection and the development of polyarteritis nodosa , a systemic necrotizing vasculitis that can affect various organ systems and can be fatal if untreated .

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not a part of HELLP syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hemolysis: This is a component of HELLP syndrome . It can be confirmed by laboratory findings such as anemia, elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and the presence of schistocytes on peripheral smear.
• Option B: Elevated liver enzymes: Elevated liver enzymes, including aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT), are represented by the EL in HELLP . They reflect hepatic injury, which is part of this syndrome.
• Option C: Thrombocytopenia: The LP in HELLP stands for low platelets , this can result in an increased risk of bleeding.
• Educational Objective:
• HELLP syndrome is characterized by Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes , and Low Platelet count , and does not include retroplacental hemorrhage as a component.

subject: Pathology
question: A nurse sustains a needle prick injury while caring for a patient with suspected hepatitis B infection. Which of the following suggests active phase of hepatitis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: IgG anti-HBc: IgG antibody to HBcAg indicates a past or chronic infection . It remains positive after an acute infection has been resolved and can also be present in chronic carriers of hepatitis B.
• Option C: IgG anti-HBs: The presence of IgG antibodies to hepatitis B surface antigen (anti-HBs) suggests immunity to hepatitis B , which can occur through vaccination or recovery from past infection. It does not indicate an active phase of infection.
• Option D: IgM anti-HBe: IgM antibody to hepatitis B e antigen (anti-HBe) is not a standard marker for the active phase of hepatitis . Anti-HBe generally indicates a lower level of viral replication and infectivity.
• Educational Objective:
• IgM anti HBc antibody becomes detectable shortly before the onset of symptoms and is a marker of the active phase of hepatitis B infection , reflecting recent exposure and active viral replication.

subject: Pathology
question: A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, dark urine, and yellow discoloration of the skin and eyes for the past week. He reports recent travel to a developing country and consuming local food and water. Physical examination reveals jaundice. Laboratory tests show elevated serum bilirubin levels, particularly the unconjugated fraction. Which hepatitis virus is most likely responsible for the maximum ground appearance change in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Hepatitis A virus can cause acute liver disease , but it is not typically associated with ground glass hepatocyte appearance . It is characterized by a self-limiting course and does not lead to chronic liver disease.
• Option C: While hepatitis C can lead to chronic liver disease and eventually to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma , it is not particularly associated with ground glass hepatocyte appearance. It typically shows more of a lymphocytic response and steatosis.
• Option D: Hepatitis E virus is another cause of acute viral hepatitis and is known for its prevalence in developing countries through contaminated water . However, it does not cause the ground glass hepatocyte appearance that is seen with hepatitis B.
• Educational Objective:
• Ground glass hepatocyte appearance is characteristic of hepatitis B infection due to the cytoplasmic accumulation of hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg).

subject: Pathology
question: The following are true attributes of hepatitis B infection except :
options: HBc Ag in serum is indicative of active infection

explanation.short: Ans. B) HBc Ag in serum is indicative of active infection
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Infants infected with the hepatitis B virus (HBV) at birth , especially from mothers with high viral loads , have a high risk of developing chronic infections because their immune systems are not fully developed to clear the virus.
• Option C: Chronic HBV infection is a known risk factor for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). The virus can integrate into the host genome and lead to mutations that contribute to oncogenesis.
• Option D: Interferons , specifically pegylated interferon , have been used in the treatment of chronic hepatitis B . They work by modulating the immune response to help control or eliminate the virus.
• Educational Objective:
• HBcAg is typically not found in the serum ; its antibodies indicate past or ongoing infection. HBeAg is a better indicator of active replication and infectivity of hepatitis B.

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old man with a history of chronic alcohol abuse presents with abdominal pain and hepatomegaly. Liver biopsy reveals abnormal intracellular inclusions in hepatocytes. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates the presence of intermediate filaments. Which protein is the major component of these inclusions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Vimentin is an intermediate filament protein found in mesenchymal cells and is not typically associated with hepatocyte inclusions. It's more commonly related to cells of mesenchymal origin.
• Option C: Keratins are a broader family of fibrous structural proteins ; cytokeratins are a subtype found in epithelial cells . In the context of liver pathology, it is more accurate to specify cytokeratins as the component of the inclusions.
• Option D: Collagen is a structural protein found in connective tissues , such as tendons and ligaments , and is not involved in the formation of Mallory bodies within hepatocytes.
• Educational Objective:
• Mallory bodies , which are abnormal intracellular inclusions found in hepatocytes , especially in conditions like alcoholic liver disease , are composed predominantly of cytokeratin intermediate filaments (CK 8 and 18).

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not associated with Mallory hyaline bodies?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Mallory bodies are associated with various liver diseases , particularly those involving chronic hepatocellular injury , but are not typically found in secondary biliary cirrhosis .

subject: Pathology
question:
A 52-year-old male with a history of diabetes mellitus and joint pains presents with skin hyperpigmentation and elevated serum ferritin. A liver biopsy is performed, and the resulting slide is shown. The patient's liver biopsy is notable for a blue staining pattern within the hepatocytes. Given below is the histopathology of liver biopsy of a patient with hemochromatosis. Which of the following stain has been used?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/08/io58.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Alcian blue: Alcian blue stains acidic polysaccharides and glycosaminoglycans in tissues blue , which is used to identify mucopolysaccharides . It is not used for iron staining.
• Option C: Von Kossa: The Von Kossa stain is used to detect calcium salts , which appear black under microscopy. This would not be the appropriate stain for identifying iron deposits in the liver.
• Option D: Crystal violet: Crystal violet is a basic dye that stains the components of cells purple or blue-violet and is often used in Gram staining of bacteria . It is not used for the detection of iron in tissue samples.
• Educational Objective:
• This stain is used to detect iron deposits in tissues and is characteristic of the pathology seen in hemochromatosis , where iron accumulation occurs within the hepatocytes.

subject: Pathology
question: True about Fibrolamellar carcinoma of Liver is all, except :
options: Females do not increased incidence than males
Serum AFP levels are usually > 1000 mg/litre

explanation.short: Ans. D) Serum AFP levels are usually > 1000 mg/liter
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Unlike conventional hepatocellular carcinoma , fibrolamellar carcinoma does not show a significant gender predilection . Both males and females are affected, and there is no clear male predominance.
• Option B: Generally, fibrolamellar carcinoma is associated with a better prognosis compared to conventional hepatocellular carcinoma . It often occurs in younger individuals and is less likely to be associated with cirrhosis . Surgical resection is the primary treatment, and if the tumor is resected early, the prognosis can be relatively favorable.
• Option C: Fibrolamellar carcinoma is characteristically not associated with liver cirrhosis . This is in contrast to conventional hepatocellular carcinoma, which is often linked to cirrhosis, especially in the setting of chronic liver diseases.
• Educational objective:
• AFP levels in fibrolamellar variant are generally not as markedly elevated as in other types of hepatocellular carcinoma

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is not correct about fibrolamellar variant of hepatocellular carcinoma?
options: Occurs in young males and females
Hepatitis B virus is an important risk factor

explanation.short: Ans. B) Hepatitis B is an important risk factor
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Fibrolamellar carcinoma can occur in both young males and females , typically in individuals without underlying liver disease or cirrhosis .
• Option C: Fibrolamellar carcinoma is generally associated with a better prognosis compared to conventional hepatocellular carcinoma .
• Option D: Fibrolamellar carcinoma can present as a firm or hard tumor , but the classic description involves a large tumor with a central scar, giving a "stellate" or "wheel spoke" appearance on imaging.
• Educational objective:
• Fibrolamellar carcinoma is not strongly associated with viral hepatitis , including hepatitis B .

subject: Pathology
question: A 60-year-old man with a history of chronic hepatitis B and cirrhosis presents with abdominal pain, unintentional weight loss, and fatigue. Imaging studies reveal a liver mass, and a biopsy confirms the diagnosis of the most common primary malignant tumor of the liver in adults. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A: Squamous cell carcinoma: Squamous cell carcinoma is a type of cancer that usually arises in the skin or tissues that line the internal organs, but it is not typically found in the liver.
• Option B: Hepatoblastoma: Hepatoblastoma is a rare malignant liver tumor that is most common in children, not adults.
• Option D: Hepatoma: Hepatoma is an older term that has been used synonymously with hepatocellular carcinoma. While it technically refers to the same cancer, the term hepatocellular carcinoma is more precise and widely used in modern medical literature.
• Educational Objective:
• It is the most common primary malignant tumor of the liver in adults and is often associated with chronic liver disease such as hepatitis B-induced cirrhosis .

subject: Pathology
question: Which is not correct about hepatocellular carcinoma?
options: Has stronger propensity to invade vascular channels
Chronic HBV has high rate of hepatocellular carcinoma

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Elevated levels of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) are commonly associated with HCC . AFP can be used as a tumor marker for HCC, and its elevation is often noted in individuals with this malignancy.
• Option C: HCC has a strong propensity to invade vascular channels , leading to features such as portal vein invasion . This invasion is associated with a poorer prognosis .
• Option D: Chronic infection with hepatitis B virus (HBV) is a significant risk factor for the development of hepatocellular carcinoma . Chronic inflammation and cirrhosis associated with HBV infection contribute to the increased risk.
• Educational objective:
• Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is more common in males than females .

subject: Pathology
question: A young woman Ms Shaano who is otherwise normal goes for an annual examination in a nursing home. Her blood investigations reveal hemoglobin is 15 gm/ dl, TLC is 7,000/mm 3 , ESR is 12 mm/hr. Her kidney and liver function tests are also normal. She undergoes a radiological scanning too. Dr. Sethi, the radiologist describes her findings to be normal except a mass in the right lobe of the liver. A biopsy is taken which confirms the diagnosis of a liver adenoma. Which of the following is likely to be associated with this lesion?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Hepatitis B may lead to hepatocellular carcinoma .
• Option A: Polycythemia vera is associated with thrombosis of the hepatic veins .
• Option D: Polyvinyl chloride , thorotrast (a contrast material) and arsenic are the risk factors for development of angio¬sarcoma of the liver . Immunohistochemical staining of these tumor cells is positive for the CD 31 cell marker.
• Educational Objective:
• Liver adenomas are benign tumors of the liver that are commonly associated with the use of oral contraceptives in young women .

subject: Pathology
question: A 65-year-old patient presents with jaundice, abdominal pain, and weight loss. Imaging studies reveal a mass lesion whose biopsy confirms the diagnosis of cholangiocarcinoma. Which of the following locations is the most common site for cholangiocarcinoma?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Cholangiocarcinoma can occur in the distal biliary tree , but perihilar tumors at the hilum are more common.
• Option C: Cholangiocarcinoma can also arise within the intrahepatic biliary ducts but this is not the commonest site.
• Option D: Cholangiocarcinoma can be multifocal , involving multiple sites along the biliary tree . However, the most common single site is the hilum.
• Educational Objective:
• Cholangiocarcinoma is a cancer arising from bile duct cells , with the most common site being the hilum of the liver , also known as perihilar or Klatskin tumors .

subject: Pathology
question: Cholangiocarcinoma of liver is caused by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Hepatitis B infection is a risk factor for hepatocellular carcinoma , but it is not a well-established risk factor for cholangiocarcinoma.
• Option B: Cirrhosis is more commonly associated with hepatocellular carcinoma rather than cholangiocarcinoma.
• Option C: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is primarily associated with lung and liver diseases , but it is not a well-established risk factor for cholangiocarcinoma.
• Educational Objective:
• Chronic infection with the liver fluke Clonorchis sinensis is a known risk factor for cholangiocarcinoma due to the chronic inflammation and damage it causes to the bile ducts.

subject: Pathology
question: A 60-year-old patient presents with jaundice, abdominal pain, and weight loss. Imaging studies reveal a mass in the bile duct. Biopsy confirms the diagnosis of the most common bile duct tumor. Which histological type is most frequently associated with tumors of the bile duct?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Tumors of the bile duct most commonly are adenocarcinomas , which originate from the glandular epithelial cells lining the bile ducts .

subject: Pathology
question: A 50-year-old male film actor Sallu Kahn looses weight rap­idly for one of his forthcoming films. He experiences occa­sional abdominal discomfort few days after that and guided by his physician, he undergoes a radiological scan (HIDA scan) is shown to have slow and incomplete gallbladder emptying in response to cholecystokinin stimulation. This patient is likely to develop which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option: Brown pigment stones are most likely to arise in cases of biliary tract infection .
• Option A: Black pigment stones are most likely to arise in cases of intravascular hemolysis.
• Option D: Gallstones do not typically contain phospho­lipid as a primary ingredient.
• Educational Objective:
• Rapid weight loss can lead to gallbladder hypomotility , resulting in the formation of biliary sludge , which is a mixture of cholesterol crystals , calcium bilirubinate , and mucus that can precede stone formation .

subject: Pathology
question: A 38-year-old patient presents with recurrent episodes of jaundice and pruritus. Liver biopsy reveals characteristic "onion skin fibrosis" around the bile ducts. Laboratory tests show elevated alkaline phosphatase levels. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with the histological finding described?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Primary biliary cirrhosis is a distinct autoimmune liver disease characterized by the destruction of small bile ducts within the liver . "Onion skin fibrosis " is not a typical histological finding in primary biliary cirrhosis .
• Option C: Extrahepatic biliary fibrosis : This term is nonspecific and not commonly used as a diagnosis.
• Option D: Congenital hepatic fibrosis is a developmental disorder of the liver and is not associated with onion skin fibrosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Primary sclerosing cholangitis is associated with characteristic "onion skin fibrosis " around bile ducts and elevated alkaline phosphatase levels , reflecting bile duct inflammation and fibrosis.

subject: Pathology
question: A 45-year-old patient with a history of chronic liver disease presents with new-onset jaundice and pruritus. The patient is concerned about the risk of developing liver cancer. Which of the following is the most significant risk factor for cholangiocarcinoma?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The most significant risk factor for cholangiocarcinoma is primary sclerosing cholangitis , which is associated with chronic inflammation and increased risk of malignancy in the bile ducts .

subject: Pathology
question: A 58-year-old patient presents with painless jaundice, pruritus, and weight loss. Imaging studies reveal a nodular mass obstructing the bile ducts at the hepatic hilum. Biopsy confirms the diagnosis of a Klatskin tumor. Which of the following best describes this tumor?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Nodular type of cholangiocarcinoma
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma: This is a distinct variant of hepatocellular carcinoma that typically affects younger individuals and has a characteristic appearance on imaging and histology, different from Klatskin tumors.
• Option C: Gall bladder carcinoma: Gall bladder carcinoma typically arises in the gall bladder itself and not in the bile ducts, hence it is not a Klatskin tumor.
• Option D: Hepatocellular carcinoma: This is a primary liver cancer that originates from hepatocytes . A Klatskin tumor, however, arises from the biliary epithelium at the confluence of the left and right hepatic bile ducts.
• Educational Objective:
• Klatskin tumors are nodular cholangiocarcinomas located at the hepatic hilum and are known for causing biliary obstruction .

subject: Pathology
question: A 25-year-old patient presents with fatigue, hepatomegaly, and neurological symptoms such as tremors and dysarthria. Ophthalmic examination reveals a brownish ring around the cornea. Laboratory tests show elevated urinary copper levels and decreased ceruloplasmin levels. Which of the following conditions is most likely responsible for the observed ophthalmic finding in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is a genetic condition that can lead to liver and lung disease due to the accumulation of abnormal alpha-1 antitrypsin protein in the hepatocytes . It does not cause the Kayser-Fleischer ring.
• Option C: Hemochromatosis: Hemochromatosis is a hereditary condition characterized by excessive iron accumulation in various organs but does not lead to the formation of a Kayser-Fleischer ring.
• Option D: Primary biliary cirrhosis: Primary biliary cirrhosis is an autoimmune liver disease characterized by the destruction of intrahepatic bile ducts . While it may lead to cirrhosis and associated symptoms, it does not cause the ophthalmic findings seen in Wilson’s disease.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of a Kayser-Fleischer ring , neurological symptoms , elevated urinary copper levels , and decreased ceruloplasmin are all diagnostic of Wilson's disease , a disorder of copper metabolism.

subject: Pathology
question: A 22-year-old patient presents with persistent jaundice and darkly pigmented liver tissue observed on imaging studies. Laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of conjugated bilirubin, and liver biopsy shows pigmented granules within hepatocytes. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the MRP2 gene. Which of the following liver disorders is most likely associated with the observed findings in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Crigler Najjar syndrome type I is characterized by severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to a deficiency of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase enzyme activity.
• Option B: Crigler Najjar syndrome type II is also characterized by unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia , but it is less severe than type I.
• Option D: Rotor syndrome is another rare disorder characterized by mild conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
• Educational Objective:
• Dubin-Johnson syndrome is characterized by conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and darkly pigmented liver due to a mutation in the MRP2 gene , leading to impaired excretion of bilirubin glucuronides.

subject: Pathology
question: A 25-year-old female presents with abdominal pain and a pelvic mass. Histological examination of the ovarian tumor reveals the presence of Schiller-Duval bodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Dysgerminoma are type of ovarian germ cell tumor , but they are characterized by monotonous sheets of large , undifferentiated cells resembling primordial germ cells .
• Option B . Teratoma are composed of multiple germ cell layers and can contain a variety of tissues such as epithelium, hair , and cartilage .
• Option D . Granulosa cell tumors are sex cord-stromal tumors , and they are characterized by the presence of Call-Exner bodies . These bodies are small, eosinophilic fluid-filled spaces surrounded by granulosa cells.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of Schiller-Duval bodies in an ovarian mass biopsy is pathognomonic for yolk sac tumors , also known as endodermal sinus tumors , and is the most likely diagnosis in a young woman presenting with these histological findings.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 30-year-old male was investigated for infertility. Semen analysis revealed azoospermia and biopsy was taken which is shown in the image. What is the likely diagnosis in the patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/28/untitled-546.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Sertoli cell only tumor
explanation.detail:
• Option A. Orchitis usually presents with signs of inflammation, not solely with the absence of germ cells as in the biopsy image.
• Option C . Testicular atrophy involves shrinkage and decreased cellular density in the seminiferous tubules, ofte accompanied by fibrosis, which is not demonstrated in the biopsy provided.
• Option D . Non-seminomatous germ cell tumors typically display malignant cells with various histologic patterns, which are not evident in the biopsy image.
• Educational Objective:
• Sertoli cell only syndrome, also known as germ cell aplasia, is diagnosed when testicular biopsy reveals seminiferous tubules with only Sertoli cells and an absence of germ cells, correlating with clinical findings of azoospermia and infertility in a male patient.

subject: Pathology
question: A 35-year-old woman undergoes a breast biopsy after a suspicious mammogram. Histological examination reveals a tumor with lack of tubule formation, discohesive cells and a low mitotic rate. Immunohistochemistry shows positive estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) staining. What is the most likely histological type of breast cancer?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Invasive ductal carcinoma tends to form glandular structures and does not usually present with the "Indian file" pattern of cell arrangement seen in invasive lobular carcinoma.
• Option C. Medullary carcinoma has a higher mitotic rate and a lymphocytic infiltrate , which differs from the low mitotic rate and discohesive cells described in the biopsy report.
• Option D . Ductal carcinoma in situ represents an early form of breast cancer that is confined to the ducts and usually forms duct-like structures, unlike the pattern described.
• Educational Objective:
• Invasive lobular carcinoma is characterized by the absence of tubule formation , discohesive cells , a low mitotic rate , and positive hormone receptor status .

subject: Pathology
question: A 47-year-old woman presents for evaluation of a hard, poorly defined mass in her left breast. She reports no pain or discharge, but she has noted a recent change in the shape of the breast. There is no family history of breast cancer. A mammogram reveals microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. A biopsy of the lesion is performed. The type of mammary ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) most likely to result in a palpable abnormality in the breast is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Apocrine DCIS presents with characteristic apocrine cytologic features under microscopy but is not the most likely to form a palpable mass.
• Option B. Neuroendocrine DCIS is a rare form and typically does not present as a palpable lesion in the breast.
• Option C. Well-differentiated DCIS may show microcalcifications on mammography , but palpable masses are less common than in the comedo subtype.
• Educational Objective:
• Comedo DCIS is most likely to present as a palpable mass due to the associated necrosis and calcifications . It is often found on mammography and can form a more solid , palpable lesion compared to other subtypes of DCIS.

subject: Pathology
question: BRCA 1 gene is located on:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Tumor-suppressor gene , BRCA-1 (17q21) encodes a zinc finger protein , and the product therefore may function as a transcription factor. The gene appears to be involved in gene repair . BRCA-2 (13q12) is also associated with an increased incidence of breast cancer in men and women .
• Educational objective :
• BRCA 1 gene is located on Chromosome 17q and BRCA2 gene is located on chromosome 13q.

subject: Pathology
question: Increased susceptibility to sporadic breast cancer is likely to be associated with a mutation in the following gene?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
